topics.title: Colour Palettes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT863 - REMOTE SENSING AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Colour Palettes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Colour Palette** (also called a **Colour Map**, **Colour Table**, or **Look-Up Table (LUT)**) is a predefined, ordered mapping function that assigns specific Red-Green-Blue (RGB) triplet values to the **Digital Numbers (DN)** stored in each pixel of a raster image. In remote sensing, raw sensor-recorded radiance values (typically integers ranging from **0 to 255** for 8-bit data, or **0 to 2047** for 11-bit data, up to **0 to 65535** for 16-bit data) are not inherently meaningful to the human eye. The colour palette acts as a **visual translator**, converting these scalar numerical intensities into perceivable chromatic and luminance variations that allow analysts to interpret land cover, vegetation vigour, water bodies, and built-up features.

In the broader context of Geographic Information Systems (GIS) and remote sensing visualization, colour palettes are categorized into three foundational classes:
- **Qualitative (Categorical) Palettes** — used for nominal data (e.g., land use classes).
- **Sequential Palettes** — used for ordered/continuous data with monotonic progression (e.g., elevation, temperature).
- **Diverging Palettes** — used for data with a meaningful midpoint (e.g., deviation from mean, NDVI).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** A colour palette is **not a property of the image data itself**; it is a property of the **display/rendering layer**. The underlying pixel DN values remain unchanged. This distinction is critical for KTU board exam answers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a **black-and-white photograph** of a thermal scene taken in a darkroom. Each pixel has a brightness number from 0 (pure black) to 255 (pure white), but the photograph is unprocessed — a flat grey smear. Now imagine you have a **box of 256 crayons**, each labelled with a brightness number. You decide to hand-colour every pixel using a specific crayon. If you use a **grayscale palette**, you essentially re-create the original photograph. If instead you use a **rainbow palette**, suddenly what was a flat grey scene becomes a vibrant thermal map where cold pixels turn blue, moderate pixels turn green/yellow, and hot pixels turn red. The **photograph never changed — only the colour interpretation did**. That box of crayons and the rule that maps each number to a crayon is the **colour palette**.

A real-world engineering analogy: consider a **car's RPM gauge**. The number of revolutions (the data) is fixed, but the gauge face can be redesigned — sporty red-gradient or conservative green-gradient. The colour palette is the *gauge face* applied to the raw *engine data*.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics and Display Constants

- **Bit-depth of a single band:** $b \in \{8, 11, 12, 16\}$ bits
- **Maximum DN value (for $b$ bits):** $DN_{max} = 2^b - 1$
- **Number of discrete grey levels (8-bit):** $2^8 = \mathbf{256}$ levels
- **Standard 24-bit True Colour channel resolution:** 8 bits per channel × 3 channels (R, G, B)
- **Reference illuminants** for display calibration: **D65** (6500 K daylight) and **D50** (5000 K horizon light)

> [!NOTE]
> A human eye can distinguish approximately **10 million colours**, while an 8-bit RGB display can render $256^3 = \mathbf{16,777,216}$ colours — sufficient to represent nearly the full perceptual gamut.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Colour Palette System

A colour palette operates through a **deterministic mapping function** $f: \mathbb{Z} \rightarrow \mathbb{Z}^3$ that takes an input integer DN and returns three 8-bit integers representing the (R, G, B) display values.

**Step 1 — Pixel Ingestion:** The renderer reads the raw DN from the raster file.
- For single-band (greyscale) data, one DN per pixel.
- For multi-band data (e.g., Landsat-8 with 11 bands), three bands are typically selected and assigned to R, G, B channels.

**Step 2 — Histogram Inspection:** A frequency histogram $H(d)$ counts the occurrences of each DN $d$ in the image. This is the foundation of intelligent palette selection.

**Step 3 — Stretching (if required):** Raw DNs are often clustered in a narrow range (e.g., 45–87 in a highly uniform scene). A **contrast stretch** expands this narrow range to fill the full 0–255 display range, dramatically improving interpretability.

**Step 4 — LUT Lookup:** Each DN is mapped to its corresponding (R, G, B) triple via the palette's lookup table.

**Step 5 — Display:** The pixel is rendered to the screen using the assigned RGB triplet.

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why stretching?** Sensors have high bit-depth (e.g., 12-bit) but most sensors rarely use the full dynamic range in a typical scene. Stretching ensures subtle differences become visible.
- **Why LUTs instead of on-the-fly computation?** LUTs are computationally efficient — a single memory access vs. complex trigonometric or polynomial calculations. They are also portable (one .clr or .txt file defines the entire palette).
- **Why three channels (R, G, B)?** Human vision is **trichromatic** — we have three types of cone cells. Display systems mimic this biology.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum DN Value | $DN_{max} = 2^b - 1$ | $b$ = bit-depth of the sensor | dimensionless integer |
| Linear Contrast Stretch | $DN'_{out} = \left\lfloor \dfrac{DN_{in} - DN_{min}}{DN_{max} - DN_{min}} \times 255 \right\rfloor$ | Maps stretched range to 0–255 | dimensionless |
| Percentage Linear Stretch | $DN'_{out} = \dfrac{DN_{in} - P_{low}}{P_{high} - P_{low}} \times 255$ | $P_{low}$, $P_{high}$ are 2nd, 98th percentile cut-offs | dimensionless |
| Histogram Equalization | $DN'_{out} = \mathrm{round}\!\left(\dfrac{CDF(DN_{in}) - CDF_{min}}{1 - CDF_{min}} \times 255\right)$ | $CDF$ = cumulative distribution function | dimensionless |
| NDVI (used with diverging palette) | $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$ | Range: $-1$ to $+1$ | dimensionless ratio |
| LUT Mapping | $(R,G,B) = LUT[DN_{in}]$ | Indexing operation | RGB triplet |
| Standard Deviation Stretch | $DN'_{out} = \dfrac{DN_{in} - (\mu - 2\sigma)}{4\sigma} \times 255$ | $\mu$ = mean, $\sigma$ = standard deviation | dimensionless |
| Display Channel Range | $D_{channel} \in [0, 255]$ | Per R, G, B channel (8-bit) | 8-bit integer |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Tip:** Always specify the **bit-depth** when discussing palette mapping. Mixing 8-bit LUTs with 16-bit raw data is a common board-exam pitfall. Use $\vert \cdot \vert$ notation (e.g., $\vert x \vert$) instead of pipe characters in tables to avoid markdown breakage.

### 2.4 Engineering Utility in Real-World Applications

- **Precision Agriculture:** NDVI maps use a **diverging palette** (red $\rightarrow$ white $\rightarrow$ green) to instantly visualize stressed vegetation.
- **Disaster Management:** Thermal palettes highlight fire fronts; elevation palettes (hypsometric tinting) reveal flood-risk zones.
- **Urban Planning:** Categorical palettes distinguish zoning classes unambiguously.
- **Oceanography:** Bathymetric sequential palettes (light cyan $\rightarrow$ dark blue) aid coral-reef mapping.
- **Mineral Exploration:** Band-ratio FCCs with custom iron-oxide palettes identify alteration zones.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Linear Percentage Contrast Stretch

The linear percentage contrast stretch is the most frequently tested stretch in KTU examinations. We will derive it from first principles.

**Statement of the Problem:**
Given an input image with raw digital numbers $DN_{in} \in [DN_{min}, DN_{max}]$, we wish to map them to the full 8-bit display range $DN'_{out} \in [0, 255]$ using a piecewise linear function.

**Step 1 — Define the Input Range**

We do not use the absolute minimum and maximum, because outliers (a single very dark or very bright pixel) can crush the rest of the contrast. Instead, we use the **2nd and 98th percentiles** to clip the bottom 2% and top 2% of pixels.

$$
P_{low} = \text{percentile}(DN_{in}, 2\%)
$$
$$
P_{high} = \text{percentile}(DN_{in}, 98\%)
$$

**Step 2 — Establish the Linear Mapping**

We desire a linear function of the form $DN'_{out} = m \cdot DN_{in} + c$, where $m$ is the slope and $c$ is the intercept.

**Step 3 — Apply Boundary Conditions**

We impose two conditions:
- When $DN_{in} = P_{low}$, then $DN'_{out} = 0$.
- When $DN_{in} = P_{high}$, then $DN'_{out} = 255$.

This yields the system:

$$
0 = m \cdot P_{low} + c
$$
$$
255 = m \cdot P_{high} + c
$$

**Step 4 — Solve the System**

From the first equation: $c = -m \cdot P_{low}$.

Substituting into the second equation:

$$
255 = m \cdot P_{high} - m \cdot P_{low} = m \cdot (P_{high} - P_{low})
$$

Solving for $m$:

$$
m = \dfrac{255}{P_{high} - P_{low}}
$$

And $c$:

$$
c = - \dfrac{255 \cdot P_{low}}{P_{high} - P_{low}}
$$

**Step 5 — Construct the Final Mapping Equation**

Substituting back into the linear form:

$$
DN'_{out} = \dfrac{255}{P_{high} - P_{low}} \cdot DN_{in} - \dfrac{255 \cdot P_{low}}{P_{high} - P_{low}}
$$

Factoring out 255:

$$
DN'_{out} = 255 \cdot \dfrac{DN_{in} - P_{low}}{P_{high} - P_{low}}
$$

**Step 6 — Floor and Clamp**

The output must be an integer in [0, 255]. We apply the floor function and clip out-of-range values:

$$
DN'_{out} = \mathrm{clip}\!\left( \left\lfloor 255 \cdot \dfrac{DN_{in} - P_{low}}{P_{high} - P_{low}} \right\rfloor, \; 0, \; 255 \right)
$$

**Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example**

Suppose $P_{low} = 48$ and $P_{high} = 192$. For an input pixel $DN_{in} = 120$:

$$
DN'_{out} = 255 \times \dfrac{120 - 48}{192 - 48} = 255 \times \dfrac{72}{144} = 255 \times 0.5 = 127.5
$$

Rounded and clipped: $DN'_{out} = \mathbf{128}$ (mid-grey).

If $DN_{in} = 200$ (above the 98th percentile):
$$
DN'_{out} = 255 \times \dfrac{200 - 48}{144} = 255 \times 1.0555 = 269.16
$$
Clipped to **255** (pure white in the display LUT).

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Palette Selection Decision Tree

| Data Type | Distribution Shape | Recommended Palette Type | Stretch Method | KTU Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Single-band panchromatic | Bell-curve, narrow range | Sequential Greyscale | Linear % stretch | Visual interpretation of pan images |
| Single-band elevation DEM | Right-skewed, long tail | Hypsometric (sequential) | Histogram equalization | Terrain visualization |
| NDVI vegetation index | Bounded in $[-1, +1]$ | **Diverging** (red-white-green) | None (raw values used) | Vegetation health |
| Land use / land cover map | Discrete classes | Qualitative (categorical) | None | Thematic mapping |
| Thermal band | Heavy left tail | Sequential (black-body) | Linear % stretch | Forest fire detection |
| Multispectral FCC | Three correlated bands | True / False Colour Composite | Often none | Landsat/Sentinel visualization |
| SAR backscatter | Log-normal distribution | Sequential (grayscale) | Logarithmic stretch | Flood mapping |

### 3.3 Histogram Equalization — Algorithmic Derivation

For an image with $N$ total pixels and a histogram $H(d)$ for digital number $d$:

**Step 1 — Compute the Probability Mass Function (PMF):**

$$
p(d) = \dfrac{H(d)}{N}, \quad \sum_{d=0}^{DN_{max}} p(d) = 1
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Cumulative Distribution Function (CDF):**

$$
CDF(d) = \sum_{i=0}^{d} p(i)
$$

**Step 3 — Normalize the CDF to 8-bit range:**

$$
CDF_{norm}(d) = \mathrm{round}\!\left( \dfrac{CDF(d) - CDF_{min}}{1 - CDF_{min}} \times 255 \right)
$$

**Step 4 — Map each pixel:**

$$
DN'_{out}(x, y) = CDF_{norm}\!\left(DN_{in}(x, y)\right)
$$

The function $CDF_{norm}$ is itself a non-linear, monotonically increasing mapping that spreads out high-frequency intensity values — a **perceptually uniform stretch**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Colour Palette Rendering Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raster Data Ingestion] --> B[Read Raw DN per Pixel]
    B --> C[Histogram Computation H of d]
    C --> D{Data Bit-Depth Match LUT?}
    D -- No --> E[Apply Contrast Stretch]
    D -- Yes --> F[Direct LUT Lookup]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Palette Type Selection]
    G --> H1[Qualitative]
    G --> H2[Sequential]
    G --> H3[Diverging]
    G --> H4[Custom FCC]
    H1 --> I[Map DN to RGB Triplet]
    H2 --> I
    H3 --> I
    H4 --> I
    I --> J[Render to Display Buffer]
    J --> K[Visible Image on Monitor]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — False Colour Composite (FCC) Band Assignment

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Multispectral Sensor] --> B1[Band 2 Blue]
    A --> B2[Band 3 Green]
    A --> B3[Band 4 Red]
    A --> B4[Band 5 NIR]
    subgraph True_Colour
        T1[B2] --> TR[Red Channel]
        T2[B3] --> TG[Green Channel]
        T3[B4] --> TB[Blue Channel]
    end
    subgraph False_Colour
        F1[B4] --> FR[Red Channel]
        F2[B3] --> FG[Green Channel]
        F3[B2] --> FB[Blue Channel]
    end
    subgraph Standard_FCC
        S1[B4 NIR] --> SR[Red Channel]
        S2[B3 Red] --> SG[Green Channel]
        S3[B2 Green] --> SB[Blue Channel]
    end
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — LUT Structure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input Pixel DN] --> B[Index into LUT]
    B --> C[LUT Row 0]
    B --> D[LUT Row 1]
    B --> E[LUT Row 2]
    B --> F[LUT Row N minus 1]
    C --> G[R0 G0 B0]
    D --> H[R1 G1 B1]
    E --> I[R2 G2 B2]
    F --> J[RN GN BN]
    G --> K[Combine into Display Pixel]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Literacy Note:** A standard `.clr` (colour) file is a plain text LUT where each line contains four integers: `DN R G B`. A 256-row LUT corresponds directly to an 8-bit image; for 16-bit data, the LUT must contain $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ entries or the DN must be rescaled first.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define a colour palette in remote sensing. Differentiate between a qualitative palette and a sequential palette with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **colour palette** is a defined mapping that assigns specific RGB colour values to the digital numbers (DN) of a raster image, enabling visual interpretation of pixel values. A **qualitative (categorical) palette** uses distinct, unordered colours to represent nominal classes (e.g., red for residential, blue for water in a land-use map). A **sequential palette** uses a graduated progression of a single hue from light to dark to represent ordered continuous data (e.g., light-to-dark blue for ocean depth). (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Look-Up Table (LUT) in the context of raster image display? Why is it preferred over on-the-fly computation?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **Look-Up Table (LUT)** is a pre-stored array of RGB triplets indexed by the pixel's digital number, allowing rapid conversion of raw DN to display colour. It is preferred because: (i) it is **computationally efficient** — only a single memory access is required per pixel, (ii) it enables **portability** — a single file can be shared and reused, and (iii) it ensures **consistency** of rendering across different software and platforms. (3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

**Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the three principal types of colour palettes — qualitative, sequential, and diverging — used in remote sensing visualization. For each type, state one application scenario and one example colour scheme.

**Model Answer:**

The three principal palette types are distinguished by the **data type** they are designed to represent.

**1. Qualitative (Categorical) Palette [2 marks]:**
This palette uses **discrete, perceptually distinct colours** with no inherent order. It is designed for **nominal data** where classes are categorically different but not ranked. Application: **Land Use / Land Cover mapping** (e.g., distinguishing forest, agriculture, urban, water as separate classes). Example: a palette assigning red to urban, green to forest, blue to water, and yellow to agriculture.

**2. Sequential Palette [2 marks]:**
This palette uses a **monotonically increasing lightness/saturation** of a single hue (or related hues) to represent **ordered, continuous data**. Application: **Digital Elevation Models (DEMs)** for terrain visualization. Example: a light-yellow $\rightarrow$ orange $\rightarrow$ dark-brown progression for low $\rightarrow$ high elevation (hypsometric tinting).

**3. Diverging Palette [3 marks]:**
This palette uses **two contrasting hues that meet at a central neutral colour** (typically white or light grey). It is ideal for data with a **meaningful midpoint**, where deviations above and below the midpoint are equally important. Application: **NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index)** maps, where values range from $-1$ to $+1$, with $0$ as the neutral midpoint. Example: a red $\rightarrow$ white $\rightarrow$ green scheme where red represents barren soil/negative NDVI, white represents bare/zero, and green represents dense healthy vegetation.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** A multispectral satellite image has the following histogram statistics for Band 4 (NIR): minimum DN = 32, 2nd percentile = 56, 98th percentile = 188, maximum DN = 240, mean = 112, standard deviation = 38. Compute the linearly stretched output DN for an input pixel with $DN_{in} = 124$ using the **2%-98% percentage linear contrast stretch**. Justify why this stretch is preferred over a min-max stretch for this dataset.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify the Stretch Parameters [1 mark]:**
We are using the **2%-98% percentage linear contrast stretch**, so:
- $P_{low} = 56$
- $P_{high} = 188$

**Step 2 — Apply the Linear Stretch Equation [3 marks]:**

$$
DN'_{out} = 255 \times \dfrac{DN_{in} - P_{low}}{P_{high} - P_{low}}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
DN'_{out} = 255 \times \dfrac{124 - 56}{188 - 56}
$$

$$
DN'_{out} = 255 \times \dfrac{68}{132}
$$

$$
DN'_{out} = 255 \times 0.5152 = 131.36
$$

**Step 3 — Round to the Nearest Integer [1 mark]:**

$$
DN'_{out} \approx \mathbf{132}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification [2 marks]:**
A **min-max stretch** would use $DN_{min} = 32$ and $DN_{max} = 240$. However, the dataset has extreme outliers — the values 32 and 240 may represent only a few anomalous pixels (sensor noise or saturated pixels). Using these would compress the contrast of the **majority of pixels** (which lie between 56 and 188) into a narrow display range, washing out the visual differentiation. The 2%-98% stretch **clips the extreme 2% of values on each tail**, ensuring the bulk of the data occupies the full 0–255 display range, yielding **superior visual contrast** for image interpretation.

---

**Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the construction of a **False Colour Composite (FCC)** from a multispectral satellite image. How does it differ from a True Colour Composite, and why is the FCC particularly useful for vegetation analysis? Illustrate with the Landsat-8 OLI band assignment.

**Model Answer:**

**True Colour Composite (TCC) [2 marks]:**
In a TCC, the three display channels (R, G, B) are assigned the bands as the human eye would perceive the scene. For Landsat-8 OLI:
- Red channel $\leftarrow$ **Band 4 (Red, 0.64–0.67 µm)**
- Green channel $\leftarrow$ **Band 3 (Green, 0.53–0.59 µm)**
- Blue channel $\leftarrow$ **Band 2 (Blue, 0.45–0.51 µm)**

The output resembles a natural photograph.

**False Colour Composite (FCC) [3 marks]:**
In a standard FCC, the Near-Infrared (NIR) band is assigned to the Red display channel. For Landsat-8 OLI:
- Red channel $\leftarrow$ **Band 5 (NIR, 0.85–0.88 µm)**
- Green channel $\leftarrow$ **Band 4 (Red, 0.64–0.67 µm)**
- Blue channel $\leftarrow$ **Band 3 (Green, 0.53–0.59 µm)**

Healthy vegetation strongly reflects NIR (due to internal leaf mesophyll structure) and absorbs red light (for photosynthesis). Thus, in an FCC, **healthy vegetation appears bright red**, water bodies appear dark blue/black (NIR absorption), urban areas appear in cyan/grey, and bare soil appears in light blue-green.

**Why FCC is Useful for Vegetation Analysis [2 marks]:**
The FCC exploits the **high contrast between NIR and Red reflectance** in vegetation. By mapping the highly reflective NIR to the most visually dominant red channel, the analyst gains **maximum visual sensitivity to vegetation density, vigour, and health**. Stress, disease, or senescence in vegetation causes a sharp drop in NIR reflectance, which is instantly visible as a colour shift from bright red to dull red, brown, or grey. This is far more sensitive than the human-perceptible green drop in TCC.

---

**(b) [7 marks]** Define a **diverging palette** and explain its application in NDVI visualization. An NDVI image has the following statistics: minimum = $-0.42$, maximum = $0.78$, mean = $0.31$, standard deviation = $0.18$. Using a **standard deviation stretch** (centre on mean, range $\mu \pm 2\sigma$), determine the NDVI value at which the diverging palette should anchor its **neutral (white) colour**.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition [2 marks]:**
A **diverging palette** is a colour scheme that uses two distinct hues diverging from a central neutral colour (typically white, light grey, or pale yellow). It is designed for data that has a meaningful central reference value, such that deviations in either direction carry symmetric informational weight.

**Step 2 — NDVI Application [2 marks]:**
NDVI is computed as $NDVI = \dfrac{NIR - Red}{NIR + Red}$, with a theoretical range of $[-1, +1]$. The midpoint $NDVI = 0$ represents the transition between non-vegetation (negative) and vegetation (positive). A diverging palette such as **red $\rightarrow$ white $\rightarrow$ green** is anchored at $NDVI = 0$: red shades indicate bare soil, water, or stressed vegetation, white represents sparse cover, and green shades represent increasingly dense and healthy vegetation.

**Step 3 — Standard Deviation Stretch Anchor [3 marks]:**
The standard deviation stretch centres the display on the mean $\mu$ and uses $\mu \pm 2\sigma$ as the dynamic range:

$$
\mu = 0.31, \quad \sigma = 0.18
$$

The dynamic range is:

$$
\mu - 2\sigma = 0.31 - 2(0.18) = 0.31 - 0.36 = -0.05
$$
$$
\mu + 2\sigma = 0.31 + 2(0.18) = 0.31 + 0.36 = 0.67
$$

The mean ($\mu = 0.31$) is the **statistical centre of the data distribution**, and is the natural choice for the **neutral (white) anchor point** of the diverging palette, because it represents the most representative NDVI value in this particular image. The diverging palette is then mapped such that NDVI = $-0.05$ corresponds to the strongest red, NDVI = $0.67$ corresponds to the strongest green, and NDVI = $0.31$ corresponds to pure white.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> (1) Do not confuse the **theoretical midpoint of NDVI (= 0)** with the **statistical mean of the image**. The question explicitly asks for the anchor based on the standard deviation stretch, which centres on the mean, not zero. (2) When stating the FCC band assignment, **always include the wavelength ranges** — examiners award marks for the spectral justification, not just the band numbers. (3) Forgetting to **floor and clip** stretched values results in display overflow and loses 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- A **colour palette** is a **display-layer mapping**, not a property of the raw raster data.
- The three primary palette types are **qualitative**, **sequential**, and **diverging** — choose based on data type (nominal, ordered, or bipolar).
- The **Look-Up Table (LUT)** indexes raw DNs to RGB triplets; bit-depth must match LUT resolution (256 entries for 8-bit).
- A **True Colour Composite** mimics human vision (R=R, G=G, B=B bands).
- A **False Colour Composite** maps **NIR $\rightarrow$ Red channel**, making healthy vegetation appear bright red.
- The **2%-98% linear stretch** is preferred over min-max when outliers exist: $DN'_{out} = 255 \cdot \dfrac{DN_{in} - P_{low}}{P_{high} - P_{low}}$.
- **Histogram equalization** uses the normalized CDF for perceptually uniform stretching.
- **NDVI** is visualized with a **red-white-green diverging palette**, anchored at the mean under a standard deviation stretch.
- Always state **bit-depth** when discussing palette mapping — a 16-bit image needs either a 65536-entry LUT or pre-scaling.
- Hypsometric tinting (sequential palette) is the standard for DEM visualization.
- Display channel range is **0 to 255** per R, G, B in 8-bit rendering.
- The standard reference illuminant for sRGB displays is **D65 (6500 K)**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical Software: SNAP
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT863 - REMOTE SENSING AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Practical Software: SNAP

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Practical Software — SNAP (Sentinel Application Platform)

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**SNAP (Sentinel Application Platform)** is an open-source common architecture for the Exploitation Monitoring (ESRIN) of the European Space Agency (ESA) Earth Observation data. It is officially defined as a collection of executable tools and Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) for the processing and analysis of **Sentinel satellite mission data**, although it is also widely used as a general-purpose remote sensing image processing software.

The software is built upon a **Java-based, platform-independent core** known as the SNAP Engine. Around this engine, several **Toolboxes** are designed — one for each Sentinel mission (S1, S2, S3, Proba-V, SMOS) — allowing domain-specific processing of Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR), Optical, and Altimetry data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **SNAP is the de-facto standard free software recommended by ESA and endorsed in the KTU PEICT863 syllabus** for hands-on implementation of remote sensing workflows. Students must be familiar with its Graph Builder, Band Maths, and classification modules.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine a **Swiss Army Knife for satellite images**. A single hardware tool (the knife body) has multiple interchangeable blades (S1, S2, S3 toolboxes). When you need to cut rope, you open the scissors blade; when you need to cut wood, you open the saw blade. Similarly, SNAP provides a single, unified interface — but when you load Sentinel-1 SAR data, the **S1 Toolbox blade** snaps in; when you load Sentinel-2 multispectral data, the **S2 Toolbox blade** snaps in. You do not have to learn five different software; one platform handles them all.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

| Term | Definition / Value |
|---|---|
| **SNAP Version** | Latest stable version is in the **9.x–10.x** series (as of 2024–25 academic cycle) |
| **Developer** | **ESA / CS-SI / Brockmann Consult** (open-source consortium) |
| **Core Language** | **Java (JDK 11+)** |
| **Data Format** | **BEAM-DIMAP (.dim)** and **NetCDF4-CF (.nc)**; also reads **GeoTIFF, HDF5, ENVISAT, JPEG2000** |
| **Distributed by** | **STEP (Science Toolbox Exploitation Platform)** portal |
| **License** | **GNU GPL v3** (fully free for academic and commercial use) |
| **Memory Footprint** | Minimum **8 GB RAM** recommended; **16 GB** for large SAR scenes |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why SNAP for KTU practicals?** The Indian Space Research Organisation (ISRO) and several NICES (National Information System for Climate and Environment Studies) training programs adopt SNAP because it is **free, cross-platform, and supports Copernicus Sentinel data** — the same data accessible freely from the Copernicus Open Access Hub.

### 1.4 Evolution & Background

- **2005–2010:** SNAP's predecessor was **BEAM (Basic Envisat Application for MERIS)** — built for ENVISAT satellite.
- **2014:** ESA rebranded and rebuilt BEAM into **SNAP** specifically to handle the incoming Sentinel constellation data (Sentinel-1A launched April 2014).
- **Today:** SNAP supports optical, SAR, and atmospheric data with consistent GUI, command-line tools, and **GPT (Graph Processing Tool)** for batch processing.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 System Architecture

SNAP follows a strict **modular, layered architecture**:

1. **SNAP Desktop (GUI layer):** Built on **NetBeans RCP (Rich Client Platform)**. Provides the visual interface.
2. **SNAP Engine (Core):** A pure Java library handling I/O, geo-coding, reprojection, masking, and pixel math.
3. **Toolbox plugins:** Mission-specific modules — **S1TBX, S2TBX, S3TBX, S3TBX (altimetry), Proba-V Toolbox, SMOS Toolbox, CHRIS Toolbox**.
4. **External plugins:** Third-party toolboxes such as **SNAP for SeaDAS (ocean color)**, **Sen2Cor** (atmospheric correction for S2), **C2RCC** (Case 2 Regional CoastColour), **SNAP-ESA CCI** toolboxes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Point:** The separation between *SNAP Engine* and *Toolbox* means that operations like *reprojection, subsetting, and band-math* are engine-level (common to all data), while operations like *S1 GRD border-noise removal* are toolbox-level (mission-specific).

## 2.2 Core Functional Capabilities

### 2.2.1 Data Import & Visualization
- Native reading of **SAFE (Standard Archive Format for Europe)** folders from Sentinel missions.
- Drag-and-drop product loading.
- Real-time **WorldWind-based 3D globe view** (via the NASA WorldWind Java SDK).
- Multiple window views: *Image View, World View, Spectrum View, Scatter Plot View, Histogram View*.

### 2.2.2 Pre-processing Operations
- **Subset (spatial/crop):** Select a region of interest.
- **Reproject:** Convert between UTM, Geographic Lat/Lon, WGS-84, sinusoidal, etc.
- **Resampling:** Bilinear, Bicubic, Nearest-Neighbour.
- **Mosaicking:** Stitch multiple tiles into a seamless mosaic.
- **Stack creation:** Multi-temporal stacking for change detection.
- **Band subsetting and band arithmetic.**

### 2.2.3 SAR-Specific Processing (S1TBX)
- **Apply Orbit File:** Updates orbit metadata with precise POD (Precise Orbit Determination) files.
- **Thermal Noise Removal:** Removes additive noise from GRD products.
- **Radiometric Calibration:** Converts digital numbers to backscatter sigma-naught (σ⁰), beta-naught (β⁰), or gamma-naught (γ⁰).
- **Speckle Filtering:** Lee, Refined-Lee, Frost, Gamma-MAP filters (window sizes 3×3, 5×5, 7×7).
- **Geometric Terrain Correction (Range-Doppler Terrain Correction):** Removes layover, foreshortening, and shadow using a DEM (SRTM 1-arcsec / 3-arcsec).
- **Coregistration:** Aligns two SAR images to sub-pixel accuracy.
- **Interferometry:** Coherence and phase unwrapping.
- **Change Detection:** Ratio, log-ratio, and coherence-based.

### 2.2.4 Optical-Specific Processing (S2TBX)
- **Sen2Cor atmospheric correction:** Bottom-of-Atmosphere (BOA) reflectance.
- **Resampling to common grid.**
- **Band arithmetic (e.g., NDVI = (B8 − B4)/(B8 + B4)).**
- **Cloud and shadow masking** (Fmask, s2cloudless plugins).
- **Land cover classification (Random Forest, K-means, Maximum Likelihood).**

### 2.2.5 Graph Builder (Workflow Automation)
- Drag-and-drop node-based workflow designer.
- Nodes = operators; edges = data flow.
- **Save as .xml graph** → execute via **GPT (Graph Processing Tool)** command-line:
  
  `gpt <graph_file>.xml -t <output>.dim -Pinput=<input>.dim`

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **GPT is a KTU favourite** for practical examinations and viva questions. The examiner frequently asks: *"How will you batch-process 50 Sentinel-1 images?"* The expected answer: build a Graph in Graph Builder, export as .xml, and run via `gpt` in a shell loop.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Operation | Formula / Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Backscatter coefficient conversion | $\sigma^0_{dB} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(\sigma^0_{linear})$ | dB (decibels) |
| 2 | Radiometric calibration (SAR) | $\sigma^0 = \frac{DN^2}{A^2} \cdot \sin(\alpha)$ | $\alpha$ = incidence angle |
| 3 | Speckle index | $SI = \dfrac{\sigma}{\mu}$ | dimensionless; $\sigma$ = std, $\mu$ = mean |
| 4 | Lee filter (simplified) | $\hat{x} = \bar{x} + K \cdot (x_c - \bar{x}), \quad K = \dfrac{\sigma_x^2}{\bar{x}^2 \sigma_v^2 + \sigma_x^2}$ | $\sigma_v^2$ = noise variance |
| 5 | NDVI | $NDVI = \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{RED}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{RED}}$ | dimensionless, range $[-1, +1]$ |
| 6 | NDWI (Water Index) | $NDWI = \dfrac{\rho_{GREEN} - \rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{GREEN} + \rho_{NIR}}$ | McFeeters 1996 |
| 7 | NDBI (Built-up Index) | $NDBI = \dfrac{\rho_{SWIR} - \rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{SWIR} + \rho_{NIR}}$ | Zha 2003 |
| 8 | SAVI | $SAVI = \dfrac{(\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{RED})}{(\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{RED} + L)} \cdot (1 + L)$ | $L$ = 0.5 (canopy correction) |
| 9 | EVI | $EVI = 2.5 \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{RED}}{\rho_{NIR} + 6\rho_{RED} - 7.5\rho_{BLUE} + 1}$ | Huete 2002 |
| 10 | Log-ratio change detection | $R_{log} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\sigma^0_{t2}}{\sigma^0_{t1}}\right)$ | dB; positive = increase |
| 11 | Coherence (InSAR) | $\gamma = \dfrac{\vert E[s_1 \cdot s_2^*] \vert}{\sqrt{E[\vert s_1\vert^2] \cdot E[\vert s_2\vert^2]}}$ | range $[0,1]$ |
| 12 | Pixel classification (Gaussian) | $P(x \mid \omega_i) = \dfrac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2} \vert \Sigma_i \vert^{1/2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{1}{2}(x-\mu_i)^T \Sigma_i^{-1} (x-\mu_i)\right)$ | used in MLC |

> [!CAUTION]
> **Markdown table syntax warning:** Note that absolute-value bars are written using `\vert` and `\mid` to prevent the table-renderer from breaking. **Never** write `|x|` inside a table row.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Agriculture:** NDVI/EVI time-series for crop health monitoring.
- **Urban planning:** Built-up extraction using NDBI and SAR coherence change.
- **Disaster management:** Flood mapping using Sentinel-1 SAR (cloud-penetrating) — critical during Kerala monsoon 2018 & 2019.
- **Defence & security:** Change detection along borders using S1 SAR.
- **Forestry:** Deforestation alerts via log-ratio of GRD backscatter.
- **Climate science:** Sea-surface temperature from S3 SLSTR; ocean colour from S3 OLCI.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Conversion of Digital Number (DN) to Backscatter ($\sigma^0$)

When a Sentinel-1 Ground Range Detected (GRD) product is opened in SNAP, the pixel values are stored as **digital numbers (DN)**. To extract geophysical meaning, the student must convert DN to calibrated backscatter using SNAP's *Radiometric Calibration* operator. Below is the rigorous derivation expected in KTU Part B answers.

### Step 1 — The Basic Radiometric Model

The SAR system measures received power $P_r$ related to the transmitted power $P_t$ via the radar equation:

$$
P_r = \frac{P_t \, G^2 \, \lambda^2}{(4\pi)^3 \, R^4} \cdot \sigma
$$

where:
- $G$ = antenna gain (dimensionless),
- $\lambda$ = radar wavelength (**~5.6 cm** for S1 C-band),
- $R$ = slant range distance (metres),
- $\sigma$ = radar cross-section (m²).

### Step 2 — Defining the Normalized Backscatter Coefficient

To make $\sigma$ independent of incidence geometry, we divide it by the ground-cell area $A$:

$$
\sigma^0 = \frac{\sigma}{A} \quad \text{(linear units, dimensionless)}
$$

The area $A$ on the ground for a pixel depends on the slant range $R$ and the local incidence angle $\alpha$:

$$
A = \frac{R \cdot \delta_a}{\sin(\alpha)}
$$

where $\delta_a$ is the azimuth resolution. Substituting:

$$
\sigma^0 = \frac{\sigma \, \sin(\alpha)}{R \, \delta_a}
$$

### Step 3 — From DN to $\sigma^0$ (SNAP's Internal Pipeline)

SNAP stores the **scaling factor** $K$ inside the product's metadata annotation file (`...annotation.xml`). The conversion in dB is:

$$
\sigma^0_{dB} = 10 \cdot \log_{10}(DN^2) - K_{dB}
$$

Equivalently, in linear units:

$$
\sigma^0_{linear} = \frac{DN^2}{K_{linear}}
$$

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example

> **Example:** A pixel in S1 GRD VH polarization has $DN = 450$ and the annotation lists the calibration constant $K_{dB} = -22.5$.

$$
\sigma^0_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(450^2) - (-22.5)
$$

$$
\sigma^0_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(202500) + 22.5
$$

$$
\sigma^0_{dB} = 10 \times 5.3065 + 22.5 = 53.065 + 22.5 = 75.565
$$

Wait — this value is implausibly high. This indicates we are working with **intensity (amplitude-squared)** DN. In SNAP, S1 GRD products are stored as **detected amplitude** (not power). The correct SNAP formula uses:

$$
\sigma^0_{linear} = \frac{DN^2}{A^2} \quad \text{where } A \text{ is the calibration amplitude LUT}
$$

SNAP's internal pipeline output, after *Apply Orbit File → Thermal Noise Removal → Calibration → Speckle Filter → Terrain Correction*, finally gives a band whose values are already in dB or linear backscatter.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** In the exam, do **not** write the radar equation unless the question explicitly asks. Instead, state the **four-step SNAP workflow** for SAR pre-processing and mention that calibration converts DN to $\sigma^0$ in dB.

## 3.2 Derivation: NDVI in Band Maths

### Step 1 — Define the Normalized Difference
$$
NDVI = \frac{NIR - RED}{NIR + RED}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Sentinel-2 Band Numbers
For S2, NIR = **Band 8 (10 m)** and RED = **Band 4 (10 m)**.

### Step 3 — Write in SNAP Band Maths Syntax

$$
NDVI = \frac{B8 - B4}{B8 + B4}
$$

In the SNAP Band Maths editor (Raster > Band Maths), the exact expression typed is:

```
(B8 - B4) / (B8 + B4)
```

The result is saved as a new band. To visualize, set the colour palette to a diverging green-red scheme, with $NDVI \in [0.0, 1.0]$ mapped to vegetation density.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SNAP Operator Decision Table

| Task | Toolbox | Operator / Menu Path | Input | Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Open SAFE folder | S1TBX | File → Open Product | `.SAFE` | `.dim` |
| Calibrate SAR | S1TBX | Radar → Radiometric → Calibrate | GRD product | Sigma0 band (dB) |
| Remove speckle | S1TBX | Radar → Speckle Filtering → Single Product Speckle Filter | Calibrated | Smoothed |
| Terrain-correct | S1TBX | Radar → Geometric → Terrain Correction → Range-Doppler | Calibrated + DEM | Geo-coded product |
| Atmospheric correction (optical) | S2TBX | Optical → Thematic Land → Sen2Cor | L1C product | L2A BOA reflectance |
| Compute NDVI | S2TBX | Raster > Band Maths | L2A bands | NDVI band |
| Classify land cover | S2TBX | Raster > Classification > Random Forest | Stacked bands | Class map |
| Time-series stack | Engine | Raster > Stack Creation | Multiple dates | Single multi-band |
| Batch process | Engine | GPT (command-line) | `.xml` graph | Automated product |
| Subset to AOI | Engine | Raster > Subset | Full scene | Clipped AOI |
| Reproject to UTM | Engine | Raster > Reproject | Native CRS | Reprojected |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Heuristic:** "Which operator did you use for ___?" is one of the most frequently asked 3-mark questions. Memorize the menu paths above.

## 3.4 Analytical Procedure: Building a Graph for Batch Flood Mapping

The following is the canonical KTU-style **practical workflow** using SNAP Graph Builder.

### Step 1 — Read the Product
Drag the **Read** node, set the `file` parameter to the SAFE folder.

### Step 2 — Apply Orbit File
Drag the *Apply-Orbit-File* node. Connect the output of *Read* to its input.

### Step 3 — Thermal Noise Removal
Connect the orbit-corrected product to *ThermalNoiseRemoval*.

### Step 4 — Radiometric Calibration
Set `outputSigmaBand=true` and `outputGammaBand=false`.

### Step 5 — Speckle Filter
Use a *Refined Lee* filter with a $5 \times 5$ window.

### Step 6 — Range-Doppler Terrain Correction
Provide the SRTM 1-arcsec DEM and specify the output projection as `EPSG:4326` (Geographic Lat/Lon).

### Step 7 — Band Maths for Water Mask

$$
WaterMask = B1 < -15.0 \;\; (\text{for } \sigma^0_{dB} \text{ in VV})
$$

### Step 8 — Write the Output
Use the *Write* node to save the final raster as GeoTIFF (`.tif`) or BEAM-DIMAP.

### Step 9 — Run via GPT

```
gpt flood_graph.xml -t Output/S1_Flood.tif -Pinput=/data/S1/S1A_*.SAFE
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 9-step pipeline above is the **standard KTU viva answer** for: *"Explain how you would map floods in Kerala using SNAP."*

The diagram below illustrates how the **SNAP Engine** interacts with mission-specific toolboxes, the GUI, and the external Graph Processing Tool.

flowchart TB

    subgraph TOOLS["MISSION TOOLBOXES"]

        T4["SMOS TBX AND PROBA V TBX"]

    subgraph PLUG["THIRD PARTY PLUGINS"]

    end

    CORE --> TOOLS
    TOOLS --> PLUG
    PLUG --> EXT

flowchart LR

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define SNAP and list any four Sentinel toolboxes available within it. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**SNAP (Sentinel Application Platform)** is an open-source software architecture developed by the **European Space Agency (ESA)** for the processing, analysis, and visualization of Earth Observation data, particularly from the Copernicus Sentinel missions. It is built in Java on the NetBeans Rich Client Platform and is distributed under the **GNU GPL v3** licence.

The four Sentinel toolboxes are:

1. **S1TBX** — Sentinel-1 SAR Toolbox (C-band SAR processing: calibration, speckle filtering, terrain correction, interferometry).
2. **S2TBX** — Sentinel-2 Optical Toolbox (multispectral processing, atmospheric correction with Sen2Cor, classification).
3. **S3TBX** — Sentinel-3 Toolbox (OLCI ocean colour, SLSTR sea/land surface temperature, altimetry).
4. **SMOS Toolbox** — for Soil Moisture and Ocean Salinity mission data.

*(Any four, 0.5 marks each; definition 1 mark = full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2: What is the Graph Processing Tool (GPT) in SNAP? Why is it useful? `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Graph Processing Tool (GPT)** is the **command-line execution engine** of SNAP. It allows users to run XML-based processing graphs (designed visually in the Graph Builder) directly from a terminal, without opening the GUI.

**Why it is useful:**

1. **Batch processing** — hundreds of products can be processed automatically in a loop.
2. **Automation** — chains can be embedded into scripts, cron jobs, or cloud pipelines.
3. **Reproducibility** — graphs are version-controlled as XML files.
4. **Headless operation** — runs on HPC servers without a display.
5. **Time-efficient** — removes GUI overhead, ideal for large SAR scenes.

A typical invocation: `gpt <graph>.xml -t <output>.dim -Pinput=<input>.dim`.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Module Internal Choice Option A

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 — Modified]**
> *(a)* Explain the layered architecture of the SNAP software. Describe the role of the SNAP Engine and the Sentinel toolboxes with a neat block diagram. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* With a step-by-step workflow, explain how you would pre-process a **Sentinel-1 GRD** product in SNAP to produce a geocoded, terrain-corrected, speckle-filtered backscatter image suitable for flood mapping. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) Architecture (7 marks)

The SNAP architecture is **three-tiered** and follows a **modular, plug-in philosophy**.

**Tier 1 — SNAP Engine (Core Java Library):**
- Provides common, mission-independent functionality.
- Handles data I/O for formats like **BEAM-DIMAP (.dim), NetCDF4-CF, HDF5, GeoTIFF, ENVISAT N1, JPEG2000**.
- Includes geo-coding routines: reprojection (UTM, Geographic, sinusoidal), resampling (Bilinear, Bicubic, NN), subsetting, mosaicking, and band math.
- Manages metadata parsing from SAFE/annotation XML files.
- Exposes a Java API to the upper tiers.

**Tier 2 — Toolboxes (Mission Plugins):**
- **S1TBX:** C-band SAR specific (calibration, speckle, geocoding, interferometry).
- **S2TBX:** Optical multispectral (Sen2Cor, classification, indices).
- **S3TBX:** OLCI + SLSTR + altimetry.
- **Proba-V TBX** and **SMOS TBX** for their respective missions.
- Each toolbox extends the engine with mission-specific operators and product readers.

**Tier 3 — Desktop GUI & GPT:**
- **SNAP Desktop:** Built on **NetBeans RCP**, providing Windows, Views (Image, World, Spectrum, Histogram), and the **Graph Builder**.
- **GPT (Graph Processing Tool):** Headless command-line invocation of the same operators defined in the engine and toolboxes.

A neat block diagram should be drawn showing **Engine → Toolbox → Desktop/GPT** with arrows indicating data flow. (A Mermaid diagram equivalent is acceptable.)

*(1 mark Engine, 1 mark Toolboxes, 1 mark Desktop/GPT, 1 mark data flow, 1 mark format support, 2 marks neat block diagram = 7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — (b) S1 GRD Workflow (7 marks)

The canonical **seven-step SNAP workflow** for a Sentinel-1 GRD product is:

1. **Read Product:** `File → Open Product → select the .SAFE folder`. The product appears as a `.dim` file in the Product Explorer.
2. **Apply Orbit File:** `Radar → Apply Orbit File`. Downloads POD precise orbit vectors from ESA to update the metadata. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Thermal Noise Removal:** `Radar → Radiometric → Thermal Noise Removal`. Removes additive sensor noise. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Radiometric Calibration:** `Radar → Radiometric → Calibrate`. Set `outputSigmaBand = true` to get $\sigma^0$ in dB. *(1 mark)*
5. **Speckle Filtering:** `Radar → Speckle Filtering → Single Product Speckle Filter`. Choose **Refined-Lee** with a $5 \times 5$ window. *(1 mark)*
6. **Range-Doppler Terrain Correction:** `Radar → Geometric → Terrain Correction → Range-Doppler`. Provide **SRTM 1-arcsec** DEM and set `pixelSpacingInMeter = 10`. *(1.5 marks)*
7. **Export & Map:** `File → Export → GeoTIFF`. Apply Band Maths with a threshold (e.g., $\sigma^0_{VV} < -15$ dB → water) to produce the **flood mask**. *(2.5 marks)*

*Full marks for naming every operator, mentioning output band names, and providing the final threshold rule for water detection.*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Module Internal Choice Option B

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 — Modified]**
> *(a)* Define NDVI. Write the mathematical expression and explain the SNAP Band Maths procedure to compute NDVI from a Sentinel-2 L2A product. **(7 marks)**
> *(b)* Compare **S1TBX (SAR)** and **S2TBX (Optical)** in terms of data type, application, atmospheric sensitivity, and processing operators. Provide two real-world use cases for each. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — (a) NDVI Computation (7 marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The **Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI)** is a dimensionless spectral index that quantifies vegetation greenness. It is computed as the difference between the **near-infrared (NIR)** and **red (RED)** reflectance, normalized by their sum:

$$
NDVI = \frac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{RED}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{RED}}
$$

For Sentinel-2, the relevant bands are **Band 8 (NIR, 10 m)** and **Band 4 (Red, 10 m)**.

**SNAP Procedure (5 marks):**

1. **Open the L2A product** (after Sen2Cor atmospheric correction): `File → Open Product → select S2A_...SAFE`.
2. **Navigate to** `Raster → Band Maths`.
3. **In the expression box**, type the formula using the band names recognized by SNAP:

```
(B8 - B4) / (B8 + B4)
```

4. **Set the variable name** to `NDVI` and click **OK**.
5. The new band appears in the Product Explorer. **Double-click** to display it in an Image View.
6. **Apply a colour palette** (`Colour Manipulation → More → Diverging green-to-red`) to visualize vegetation density: green for $NDVI > 0.6$, brown for $NDVI < 0.2$.
7. **Export** as GeoTIFF via `File → Export → GeoTIFF` for further GIS integration.

*Full marks for definition (2) + correct formula (1) + accurate Band Maths path (2) + interpretation/palette (2) = 7 marks.*

#### Model Solution — (b) Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Feature | **S1TBX (SAR)** | **S2TBX (Optical)** |
|---|---|---|
| Data type | C-band microwave, **5.405 GHz** | Visible + SWIR multispectral, 13 bands |
| Wavelength | **~5.6 cm** (microwave) | **0.443 – 2.190 µm** (optical) |
| Cloud penetration | **Yes** — works day/night, all-weather | **No** — affected by clouds & haze |
| Atmospheric sensitivity | Low (microwaves barely interact) | **High** — needs atmospheric correction |
| Sun illumination | Not required (active sensor) | Required (passive sensor) |
| Typical operators | Orbit file, calibration, speckle filter, terrain correction, interferometry | Sen2Cor, classification, band math, NDVI, time-series stack |
| Output backscatter / reflectance | $\sigma^0$, $\beta^0$, $\gamma^0$ in **dB** | Surface reflectance $\rho$ (unitless) |
| Ground resolution | **5 m** (IW mode SLC), **10 m** (GRD) | **10 m, 20 m, 60 m** (band-dependent) |

**Real-world use cases (2 marks each pair, 0.5 per case):**

*S1TBX use cases:*
- **Flood mapping in Kerala** during the 2018 monsoon (cloud-penetrating SAR revealed inundated areas when optical imagery was blocked by clouds).
- **Ship detection** in the Arabian Sea using constant false-alarm rate (CFAR) on S1 GRD.

*S2TBX use cases:*
- **Crop health monitoring** in Palakkad using NDVI/EVI time-series.
- **Urban sprawl mapping** in Bengaluru using NDBI (Normalized Difference Built-up Index).

*(1 mark table + 1 mark each tool + 2 marks for cases = 7 marks total.)*

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where KTU students lose marks on SNAP questions:**
> 1. **Confusing S1TBX and S2TBX terminology** — e.g., writing "NDVI for SAR" or "speckle in optical". This is an **automatic 0.5 mark cut** per error.
> 2. **Writing `SRTM 30 m` as the DEM for S1 terrain correction** — SRTM 1-arcsec (~30 m) is fine, but ESA recommends the **1-arcsec SRTM v3** specifically. Quote the version.
> 3. **Forgetting the units of $\sigma^0$** — always state whether you are in **linear** or **dB**.
> 4. **Typing wrong band names in Band Maths** — SNAP is case-sensitive in some operators; use `B8`, `B4` (uppercase B, no space).
> 5. **Stating "GPT is a Python tool"** — wrong; it is a **Java command-line** tool. Severe 1-mark penalty.
> 6. **Drawing the architecture diagram without arrows** — Examiner awards 1 mark specifically for **labelled arrows** indicating data flow.
> 7. **Skipping the speckle-filter step** — Calibration + Terrain Correction alone is **not** enough; speckle filtering is a separate, mandatory 1-mark operator.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **SNAP — Rapid-Revision Checklist (PEICT863, Module 2):**

- **SNAP** = Sentinel Application Platform; **open-source, Java-based, GPL-3**; developed by **ESA + CS-SI + Brockmann Consult**.
- Built on the **NetBeans RCP**; native data format is **BEAM-DIMAP (.dim)**; supports **SAFE, NetCDF, GeoTIFF, HDF5**.
- **Core = SNAP Engine** (mission-independent I/O, geo-coding, band math).
- **Surrounding toolboxes** = S1TBX (SAR), S2TBX (Optical), S3TBX (OLCI/SLSTR/Altimetry), Proba-V TBX, SMOS TBX.
- **Standard SAR pre-processing chain (memorize the 7 steps):**
  Read → Apply Orbit File → Thermal Noise Removal → Radiometric Calibration → Speckle Filter → Range-Doppler Terrain Correction → Export.
- **NDVI = (B8 − B4) / (B8 + B4)** for Sentinel-2; range $[-1, +1]$.
- **GPT (Graph Processing Tool)** runs XML graphs from the command line: `gpt <graph>.xml -t <output>`.
- **Graph Builder** = visual workflow designer; nodes are operators, edges are data flow.
- **Key SAR formulae:** $\sigma^0_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(DN^2/A^2)$; $SI = \sigma/\mu$; $\gamma$ = coherence.
- **Key optical formulae:** NDVI, NDWI, NDBI, SAVI, EVI — all expressible in SNAP **Band Maths** editor.
- **Sentinel-1 C-band λ ≈ 5.6 cm**; works day/night, all-weather — **the big advantage over optical**.
- **Sentinel-2 has 13 bands**; **Band 8 = NIR (10 m)**, **Band 4 = Red (10 m)**, **Band 2 = Blue**, **Band 11 = SWIR (20 m)**.
- **Kerala flood 2018** is the canonical KTU case study for S1 SAR flood mapping.
- **Examiner's quick-mark operators** — know menu paths for *Calibrate*, *Speckle Filter*, *Terrain Correction*, *Band Maths*, *Sen2Cor*.
- **Always state units** in your answer: dB for SAR backscatter, unitless for NDVI, degrees for incidence angle, metres for resolution.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Downloading
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT863 - REMOTE SENSING AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Downloading

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Downloading in Remote Sensing** refers to the systematic process of acquiring digital Earth observation data — including satellite imagery, aerial photographs, and ancillary geospatial datasets — from public or commercial archives (data centres, cloud platforms, or FTP servers) over the internet using standardised protocols (HTTP, FTP, OPeNDAP, AWS S3), following user-defined search criteria such as geographic extent, temporal window, sensor type, and cloud-cover threshold.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, the **Downloading** module bridges the gap between the *theoretical understanding* of remote sensing platforms (covered in Module 1) and the *practical data acquisition* that underpins every geospatial project — from land-use classification to change-detection analysis.

### 1.2 Key Data Repositories (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

| Repository | Full Name | Owner | Data Hosted |
|---|---|---|---|
| **USGS EarthExplorer** | United States Geological Survey EarthExplorer | NASA / USGS | Landsat 1–9, SRTM, ASTER, MODIS |
| **Copernicus Browser** | Copernicus Open Access Hub | ESA | Sentinel-1, 2, 3, 5P |
| **GloVis** | Global Visualization Viewer | USGS | Landsat, Sentinel-2 (visual) |
| **NASA Earthdata** | NASA Earth Observing System Data | NASA | MODIS, VIIRS, SMAP |
| **Bhuvan** | Indian Geo-platform | ISRO | Resourcesat, Cartosat, MOSDAC |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Under the **KTU 2024 Scheme**, students are expected to demonstrate hands-on competency in navigating at least **two** of the above portals and executing a real data-download workflow as part of their continuous assessment (CO2 — *Apply*).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you want to study how a city has grown over ten years. The satellite has been dutifully **photographing the Earth every day**, storing trillions of pixels in massive digital vaults. **Downloading** is the equivalent of walking into a colossal library, telling the librarian *"I need all photos of Kochi between 2014–2024, taken on cloudless afternoons, by a specific camera"*, and having those specific files copied to your USB drive.

The librarian (the data portal) uses a **search engine**; you submit a **query** (coordinates, date, sensor); the portal returns a **catalogue** (list of matching scenes); you **preview** them; you finally **transfer** the chosen files to your local machine.

### 1.4 Fundamental Terminology

- **Scene / Granule:** A single, contiguous image tile captured during one satellite overpass.
- **Metadata:** Descriptive information embedded in or shipped alongside the image (acquisition date, sun elevation, cloud cover, projection).
- **Thumbnail:** Low-resolution preview rendered in the portal for quick visual screening.
- **Bulk Download:** Automated retrieval of many scenes using a script (Python, API call, wget).
- **API Key:** A unique authentication token required by portals like Copernicus and NASA Earthdata.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** Whenever a question asks *"List the steps to download data"*, always structure your answer in **four phases** — *Search → Filter → Preview → Retrieve*. Examiners award marks for logical sequencing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Four-Phase Download Workflow

A complete remote-sensing data download, whether for academic projects or industrial pipelines, follows a deterministic four-phase protocol:

#### Phase 1 — Search (Query Construction)
The user defines the **Area of Interest (AOI)** by:
- Uploading a shapefile / KML, or
- Drawing a bounding box on a web map, or
- Specifying path/row coordinates (legacy Landsat WRS-2 system).

The user further specifies:
- **Sensor / Platform:** e.g., Landsat-8 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI
- **Date Range:** start and end timestamps
- **Cloud Cover Threshold:** typically 0–10 % for optical data

#### Phase 2 — Filter (Catalogue Refinement)
The portal's backend database returns all granules matching the query. Filters applied at this stage include:
- **Spatial overlap** with AOI
- **Acquisition date** within range
- **Cloud cover percentage** ≤ threshold
- **Sensor mode / Product level** (e.g., L1C, L2A, Surface Reflectance)

#### Phase 3 — Preview (Quality Screening)
Thumbnails and quick-look images are inspected. This avoids wasting bandwidth on cloud-obscured or off-target scenes.

#### Phase 4 — Retrieve (File Transfer)
The user initiates the actual transfer. The data arrives in a **standardised container** such as a `.tar.gz` archive or a directory of `.tif` + `.xml` files.

### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Matters | What Happens If Skipped |
|---|---|---|
| Search | Narrows millions of global scenes to a few hundred | Download is impossibly slow / impractical |
| Filter | Eliminates scenes with >threshold cloud cover | Atmospheric contamination corrupts classification |
| Preview | Confirms scene covers AOI | Wasted bandwidth on wrong geographic tiles |
| Retrieve | Transfers the actual pixels to your disk | No data = no analysis |

### 2.3 KTU Formula / Parameter Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Although "Downloading" is largely procedural, the following **quantitative relationships** frequently appear in KTU theory questions.

| Parameter / Concept | Equation or Value | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| File size of one Landsat-8 scene | ≈ **0.7 – 1.0 GB** | GB | Compressed |
| File size of one Sentinel-2 L2A tile | ≈ **800 MB** | MB | `.SAFE` folder |
| Number of bands in Landsat-8 OLI | **11** | — | 8 spectral + 1 pan + 2 thermal |
| Number of bands in Sentinel-2 MSI | **13** | — | 10 m, 20 m, 60 m resolutions |
| Spatial resolution of Landsat-8 | **30 m** (multispectral) | m | 15 m panchromatic |
| Spatial resolution of Sentinel-2 | **10 m, 20 m, 60 m** | m | Band-dependent |
| Revisit period of Landsat-8 | **16 days** | days | Single satellite |
| Revisit period of Sentinel-2 | **5 days** | days | Twin-satellite constellation |
| Cloud-cover threshold formula | $CC_{scene} \le CC_{threshold}$ | % | Used in filter |
| Estimated download time | $T_{d} = \dfrac{S}{B \times \eta}$ | seconds | $S$ = file size, $B$ = bandwidth, $\eta$ = efficiency (0.6–0.8) |

**Key formula expansion for download-time estimation:**

$$
T_{d} = \frac{S}{B \times \eta}
$$

Where:
- $T_{d}$ = download time (seconds)
- $S$ = file size (bits or bytes — ensure unit consistency)
- $B$ = nominal bandwidth (bits/sec or bytes/sec)
- $\eta$ = protocol efficiency factor, typically **0.6 – 0.8** for HTTP/FTP

> [!NOTE]
> A common KTU numerical: *"Estimate the time to download a 1.2 GB Landsat scene over a 50 Mbps connection with 70 % efficiency."* Practice this type of unit conversion carefully.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application |
|---|---|
| **Agriculture** | Downloading NDVI time-series for crop monitoring |
| **Urban Planning** | Sentinel-2 tiles for built-up area delineation |
| **Disaster Management** | Rapid Sentinel-1 SAR downloads for flood mapping |
| **Forestry** | Landsat archive for deforestation tracking |
| **Climate Science** | MODIS global products for temperature anomaly studies |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Sample KTU-Style Numerical Problem (Worked Out)

> **Problem:** *A researcher needs to download 10 Sentinel-2 L2A tiles for a study area. Each tile is 800 MB. The available internet connection is 100 Mbps (megabits per second), and the protocol efficiency is 75 %. Calculate the total download time in minutes.*

#### Step 1 — Compute total file size

$$
S_{total} = N \times S_{tile}
$$

$$
S_{total} = 10 \times 800 \text{ MB} = 8000 \text{ MB}
$$

Convert to megabits (since bandwidth is in megabits per second):

$$
S_{total\ (Mbit)} = 8000 \times 8 = 64{,}000 \text{ Mbit}
$$

#### Step 2 — Apply the bandwidth × efficiency factor

$$
B_{effective} = B \times \eta
$$

$$
B_{effective} = 100 \times 0.75 = 75 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

#### Step 3 — Compute download time in seconds

$$
T_{d} = \frac{S_{total\ (Mbit)}}{B_{effective}}
$$

$$
T_{d} = \frac{64{,}000}{75} = 853.33 \text{ seconds}
$$

#### Step 4 — Convert to minutes

$$
T_{d\ (min)} = \frac{853.33}{60} = 14.22 \text{ minutes}
$$

> **Final Answer:** $T_{d} \approx 14.22$ minutes

#### Step 5 — Sanity Check (Analytical Matrix)

| Quantity | Value | Unit Check |
|---|---|---|
| 10 tiles × 800 MB | 8000 MB | ✓ |
| 8000 MB × 8 | 64,000 Mbit | ✓ |
| 100 × 0.75 | 75 Mbit/s | ✓ |
| 64,000 / 75 | 853.33 s | ✓ |
| 853.33 / 60 | 14.22 min | ✓ |

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU Pitfall:** Students forget to convert **MB → Mbit** by multiplying by 8. This single error wipes out 2–3 marks in the valuation key.

### 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Three Major Data Portals

| Feature | USGS EarthExplorer | Copernicus Browser | NASA Earthdata |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Login Required** | Yes (free) | Yes (free) | Yes (free) |
| **Bulk Download** | Limited (via bulk order app) | Yes (scripted API) | Yes (via `earthaccess`) |
| **API Available** | Yes (EE API) | Yes (OData) | Yes (CMR) |
| **Cloud-Native Access** | Limited | Yes (AWS / CreoDIAS) | Yes (AWS S3) |
| **Best For** | Landsat, ASTER, SRTM | Sentinel family | MODIS, VIIRS, SMAP |
| **Storage Format** | GeoTIFF (`.tif`) | JPEG2000 / SAFE | HDF5 / NetCDF |

### 3.3 Practical Cloud-Cover Threshold Derivation

Often, the analyst must decide how many scenes to accept from a filtered list. A useful relationship is:

$$
N_{usable} = N_{total} \times P(CC \le CC_{max})
$$

Where:
- $N_{total}$ = total scenes returned by the search
- $P(CC \le CC_{max})$ = probability that a random scene has cloud cover below the maximum threshold (empirically derived from the catalog statistics).

In practice, if 120 scenes are returned and 25 satisfy $CC \le 10\,\%$:

$$
N_{usable} = 120 \times \left(\frac{25}{120}\right) = 25 \text{ scenes}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram — Complete Downloading Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Analyst Defines Objective] --> A1[Select Data Portal]
    A1 --> A2[USGS EarthExplorer]
    A1 --> A3[Copernicus Browser]
    A1 --> A4[NASA Earthdata]
    A1 --> A5[Bhuvan]
    A2 --> B1[Register and Login]
    A3 --> B1
    A4 --> B1
    A5 --> B1
    B1 --> C1[Define AOI Shapefile or Bounding Box]
    C1 --> C2[Set Date Range]
    C2 --> C3[Set Cloud Cover Threshold]
    C3 --> D1[Run Search Query]
    D1 --> E1[Filter Results by Sensor and Product Level]
    E1 --> F1[Preview Thumbnails]
    F1 --> G1{Scene Acceptable}
    G1 -->|No| E1
    G1 -->|Yes| H1[Add to Cart or Download Basket]
    H1 --> I1[Choose Download Method]
    I1 --> J1[Direct HTTP or FTP]
    I1 --> J2[API Script]
    I1 --> J3[Cloud Bucket Access]
    J1 --> K1[File Arrives as TAR or SAFE Folder]
    J2 --> K1
    J3 --> K1
    K1 --> L1[Verify Checksum and Metadata]
    L1 --> M1[Store in Local or Cloud Archive]
    M1 --> N1[Ready for Preprocessing]
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Data Acquisition Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SRC[Data Source Layer]
        S1[Satellite Sensors]
        S2[Ground Receiving Stations]
        S3[Archive Catalogs]
    end

    subgraph PORT[Portal Layer]
        P1[Search Engine]
        P2[Metadata Database]
        P3[Authentication Service]
    end

    subgraph XFER[Transfer Layer]
        T1[HTTP Protocol]
        T2[FTP Protocol]
        T3[API Endpoint]
        T4[Cloud Storage S3]
    end

    subgraph CLIENT[Client Layer]
        C1[Web Browser]
        C2[Python Script]
        C3[GIS Software QGIS ArcGIS]
    end

    subgraph STOR[Storage Layer]
        Z1[Local Hard Disk]
        Z2[Network Attached Storage]
        Z3[Cloud Bucket]
    end

    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
    S3 --> P2
    P1 --> P2
    P3 --> PORT
    P1 --> T1
    P1 --> T2
    P1 --> T3
    P1 --> T4
    T1 --> C1
    T2 --> C2
    T3 --> C2
    T4 --> C3
    C1 --> Z1
    C2 --> Z2
    C3 --> Z3
```

### 4.3 Sequence Diagram — Bulk Download via API

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant U as User
    participant S as Search Portal
    participant C as Catalogue DB
    participant A as Auth Server
    participant F as File Server

    U->>S: Submit AOI + Filters
    S->>A: Validate Credentials
    A-->>S: Token Issued
    S->>C: Query Scenes Matching Criteria
    C-->>S: List of Granules with Metadata
    S-->>U: Display Results
    U->>S: Select Scenes and Initiate Download
    S->>F: Request File Transfer
    F-->>U: Streamed Data (HTTP/FTP)
    U->>U: Verify MD5 Checksum
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> **Q1. Define the term "Downloading" in the context of remote sensing. List any four popular data portals used for satellite data download.**

**Model Answer:**
Downloading in remote sensing is the process of acquiring digital Earth observation data from online archives and portals, typically over the internet, by specifying search criteria such as geographic location, time period, sensor type, and acceptable cloud-cover level.

Four popular portals:
1. **USGS EarthExplorer** — for Landsat, ASTER, SRTM
2. **Copernicus Browser** — for Sentinel family data
3. **NASA Earthdata** — for MODIS, VIIRS, SMAP
4. **Bhuvan (ISRO)** — for Indian satellite missions

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> **Q2. What is the significance of "Metadata" in downloaded remote sensing datasets?**

**Model Answer:**
Metadata is descriptive information embedded within or shipped alongside an image file. It contains critical parameters such as acquisition date and time, sensor type, sun azimuth and elevation, cloud cover percentage, spatial resolution, projection system, and processing level. Metadata allows the analyst to:
- Verify the scene suits the project requirements
- Correctly georeference the image
- Apply the correct atmospheric or radiometric calibration parameters
- Cite the data source scientifically

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Module 2, Internal Choice Pattern]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **Q.A (a)** Explain in detail the step-by-step procedure for downloading a Landsat-8 OLI scene from USGS EarthExplorer, including the search filters commonly applied. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Registration & Login:** Visit `earthexplorer.usgs.gov` and create a free account using institutional email.

2. **Define Search Criteria:**
   - *Search Criteria Tab:* Enter coordinates of the AOI (lat/long or upload shapefile) or use the WRS-2 Path/Row system.
   - *Date Range Tab:* Specify start and end dates (e.g., 2020-01-01 to 2024-12-31).
   - *Cloud Cover Tab:* Set maximum cloud cover (e.g., 10 %).

3. **Select Dataset:** Navigate to *Data Sets* → *USGS Landsat* → *Landsat Collection 2 Level-1* → *Landsat 8-9 OLI/TIRS C2 L1*.

4. **Additional Criteria (optional):** Filter by day/night, cloud cover range, sensor identifier.

5. **Result Display:** Click *Results*; the portal returns thumbnails of all matching scenes.

6. **Preview & Select:** Click on thumbnails to view quick-look browse images. Verify the AOI is covered, and cloud cover is acceptable.

7. **Download Options:**
   - *Level-1 GeoTIFF Data Product* — large, full-quality file.
   - *Level-1 Bundle* — includes all bands.
   - *Download* button initiates direct transfer (free with login).

8. **Bulk Download App:** For multiple scenes, use the *Bulk Download Application* which queues files for later automated retrieval.

> **Q.A (b)** A researcher wants to download 5 Sentinel-2 L2A tiles (each 800 MB) over a 60 Mbps broadband connection. The protocol efficiency is 70 %. Estimate the total download time in minutes. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$$
S_{total} = 5 \times 800 = 4000 \text{ MB}
$$

Converting to megabits:

$$
S_{total\ (Mbit)} = 4000 \times 8 = 32{,}000 \text{ Mbit}
$$

Effective bandwidth:

$$
B_{eff} = 60 \times 0.70 = 42 \text{ Mbit/s}
$$

Download time:

$$
T_d = \frac{32{,}000}{42} = 761.90 \text{ seconds}
$$

In minutes:

$$
T_d = \frac{761.90}{60} \approx 12.70 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Final Answer:** $T_d \approx 12.70$ minutes.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

> **Q.B (a)** Compare the features of three major remote sensing data portals — USGS EarthExplorer, Copernicus Browser, and NASA Earthdata — under the following heads: data hosted, login requirement, bulk download facility, and cloud-native access. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Feature | USGS EarthExplorer | Copernicus Browser | NASA Earthdata |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Data Hosted** | Landsat 1–9, ASTER, MODIS, SRTM, Sentinel-2 (via collaboration) | Sentinel-1, 2, 3, 5P, Sentinel-6 | MODIS, VIIRS, SMAP, GPM, CALIPSO |
| **Login Required** | Yes (free) | Yes (free) | Yes (free) |
| **Bulk Download Facility** | Yes (Bulk Download App) | Yes (OData / Python API) | Yes (CMR API + `earthaccess` library) |
| **Cloud-Native Access** | Limited | Yes (Copernicus Data Space, AWS) | Yes (AWS S3, Google Earth Engine exports) |

Conclusion: Copernicus and NASA Earthdata lead in cloud-native infrastructure, while USGS remains the authoritative archive for Landsat data.

> **Q.B (b)** What are the different file formats encountered when downloading remote sensing data? Explain the structure of the SAFE format used by Sentinel-2. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Common formats:
- **GeoTIFF (`.tif`)** — georeferenced raster; used by Landsat, ASTER.
- **HDF5 / HDF-EOS (`.h5`, `.hdf`)** — hierarchical data; used by MODIS.
- **NetCDF (`.nc`)** — array-oriented scientific data; used for climate datasets.
- **JPEG2000 (`.jp2`)** — used inside Sentinel-2 SAFE.
- **SAFE (`.SAFE`)** — folder structure, not a single file; used by Sentinel.

**Sentinel-2 SAFE Folder Structure:**

```
S2A_MSIL2A_20240315T045651_N0510_R092_T43PGQ_20240315T082920.SAFE
│
├── GRANULE
│   └── L2A_T43PGQ_A045651_20240315T045651
│       ├── IMG_DATA
│       │   ├── R10m
│       │   │   ├── T43PGQ_20240315_B02_10m.jp2
│       │   │   ├── T43PGQ_20240315_B03_10m.jp2
│       │   │   ├── T43PGQ_20240315_B04_10m.jp2
│       │   │   └── T43PGQ_20240315_B08_10m.jp2
│       │   ├── R20m
│       │   │   ├── T43PGQ_20240315_B05_20m.jp2 ... B12_20m.jp2
│       │   └── R60m
│       │       ├── T43PGQ_20240315_B01_60m.jp2 ... B09_60m.jp2
│       ├── QI_DATA
│       │   ├── MSK_CLOUDS_B00.gml
│       │   └── AUX_PREDEF_AOT.gml
│       └── MTD_TL.xml
├── AUX_DATA
├── DATASTRIP
│   └── DS_PRD_MSIL2A_20240315T045651
│       ├── QI_DATA
│       └── MTD_DS.xml
├── INSPIRE.xml
├── manifest.safe
└── rep_info
    ├── L2A_Report.xml
    └── L2A_User_Product_Report.xml
```

Key elements:
- **GRANULE/** — contains per-tile imagery at three resolutions (10 m, 20 m, 60 m).
- **MTD_TL.xml** — tile-level metadata.
- **QI_DATA/** — quality indicators (cloud mask, snow mask, AOT, water vapour).
- **manifest.safe** — main XML index of the product.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting unit conversion (MB ↔ Mbit)** — multiply bytes by 8 to get bits. This alone costs 2–3 marks in numerical questions.
> 2. **Vague step descriptions** — Examiners want *concrete portal names* (e.g., "Open Copernicus Browser" not just "go to a website").
> 3. **Skipping metadata verification** — always mention that you must check the `.xml` or `manifest.safe` file after download.
> 4. **Confusing file sizes** — Landsat-8 ≈ 1 GB; Sentinel-2 ≈ 800 MB. Mixing these up loses marks.
> 5. **Ignoring the bulk download option** — for projects requiring many scenes, mentioning API / scripted download earns extra credit.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Downloading** = acquiring satellite / aerial data from web portals over the internet.
- The four-phase workflow is **Search → Filter → Preview → Retrieve**.
- **USGS EarthExplorer**, **Copernicus Browser**, and **NASA Earthdata** are the three most important KTU-recommended portals.
- **Bhuvan** is the Indian equivalent for ISRO missions.
- File formats: **GeoTIFF, HDF5, NetCDF, JPEG2000, SAFE folder**.
- Landsat-8 scene ≈ **1 GB**; Sentinel-2 L2A tile ≈ **800 MB**.
- Cloud-cover threshold: typically **≤ 10 %** for optical sensor analysis.
- Download time formula: $T_d = \dfrac{S}{B \times \eta}$ — always convert units carefully.
- **Metadata** is non-negotiable: always inspect `manifest.safe`, `MTD_TL.xml`, or `*.xml` after download.
- **Bulk download** is enabled via API scripts (Python `sentinelsat`, `earthaccess`, or `wget`).
- **CO Alignment:** This topic maps to **CO2 (Apply)** under the KTU 2024 Scheme — students must demonstrate *practical hands-on download competency*.
- **Bloom's Level:** Mostly **Apply** and **Analyse** — expect scenario-based questions in Part B.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Importing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEICT863 - REMOTE SENSED AND CONTROL
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Importing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Data Importing** in the context of Remote Sensing and GIS (Geographic Information Systems) refers to the systematic process of bringing external geospatial datasets—such as satellite imagery, aerial photographs, vector layers, GPS readings, and ancillary tabular data—into a software environment (GIS or image-processing workstation) for subsequent analysis, manipulation, and interpretation. The import operation typically includes **format translation**, **coordinate reference system (CRS) recognition/assignment**, **metadata extraction**, and **spatial registration** of the data into the project's working geodatabase.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Importing is the *first operational step* in the Remote Sensing data workflow. The quality of import directly governs the integrity of all downstream processes—radiometric correction, classification, and change detection.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of **importing** like **moving furniture into a new house**:

- The **furniture (data)** comes from many different stores (satellite sensors, GPS devices, survey teams). Each store packages it differently (GeoTIFF, Shapefile, CSV).
- The **moving truck (import wizard)** translates the package format, places the furniture into the right rooms (layers), and labels them so you can find them later (metadata + CRS).
- If the moving company misplaces your furniture or sets up the wrong room address (wrong CRS/projection), every later activity—cooking, sleeping, working—will be chaotic.

### Why "Importing" Matters in Remote Sensing

Remote Sensing projects involve **multi-source, multi-format, multi-resolution** datasets. Without a structured import process:

- **Spatial misalignment** between layers causes geometric errors.
- **Format incompatibility** prevents software from reading files.
- **Lost metadata** removes radiometric and geometric calibration parameters essential for scientific analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Standard Metrics to Remember:**
> - **Spatial Resolution:** typically **0.5 m – 30 m** for commercial earth-observation satellites.
> - **Spectral Resolution:** 4–14+ bands (multispectral to hyperspectral).
> - **Standard Data Formats:** GeoTIFF, ERDAS IMAGINE (.img), ENVI (.dat), HDF5, NetCDF, Shapefile (.shp), KML/KMZ, GeoJSON.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Importing Workflow — Step-by-Step Logic

The remote sensing import workflow is governed by a structured sequence of operations:

1. **Source Identification & Inventory**
   - Identify the sensor (e.g., Landsat 8/9 OLI, Sentinel-2 MSI, Resourcesat LISS-III, Cartosat).
   - Catalog the file format and accompanying metadata (.xml, .json, .hdr, .tfw world file).

2. **Format Compatibility Check**
   - Verify whether the software (QGIS, ArcGIS Pro, ERDAS Imagine, ENVI) natively supports the format or requires a driver/plugin (e.g., GDAL/OGR library).

3. **Coordinate Reference System (CRS) Handling**
   - **Auto-detect** CRS from embedded metadata (e.g., GeoTIFF tags, .prj file).
   - **On-the-fly reprojection** if the project CRS differs from the source CRS.
   - **Assign CRS manually** when none is embedded (common with raw GPS exports).

4. **Data Translation & Loading**
   - Translation occurs through libraries like **GDAL** (raster) and **OGR** (vector).
   - Raster data is converted into band-interleaved-by-pixel (BIP), band-interleaved-by-line (BIL), or band-sequential (BSQ) structures as required by the software.

5. **Metadata Embedding**
   - Attribute the imported layer with descriptive tags: acquisition date, sensor, cloud cover %, spatial reference.

6. **Validation & Visualization**
   - Overlay on basemaps, check alignment, verify band statistics, ensure no NULL/no-data gaps.

### 2.2 Supported Data Formats — Cheat Sheet

| Data Type | Common Formats | Typical Use | CRS Info Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Satellite Raster | GeoTIFF, .img, .dat, HDF5, NetCDF | Multispectral/Hyperspectral imagery | Embedded in file header |
| Aerial Raster | TIFF, ECW, MrSID, JPEG2000 | Orthophotos | .tfw world file / embedded |
| Vector | Shapefile (.shp), GeoJSON, KML, GPKG | Administrative boundaries, roads | .prj file |
| GPS / Tabular | CSV, GPX, TXT | Field survey points | Manual assignment |
| LiDAR Point Cloud | LAS, LAZ | 3D elevation models | .prj or .slas |
| Digital Elevation | GeoTIFF, ASCII Grid | DEM, DSM, DTM | Embedded / world file |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Aid:** A **World File** (e.g., `.tfw`, `.jgw`) is a 6-line text file containing the affine transformation parameters (pixel size in X, rotation, origin coordinates). It is the most common "side-car" file used when the raster format itself does not natively store georeferencing.

### 2.3 The Affine Transformation (World File Mathematics)
