# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define NDVI and explain its significance in vegetation analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

NDVI (Normalized Difference Vegetation Index) is a spectral index computed from the red and near-infrared (NIR) bands of a multispectral image. The formula is:

$$
\text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}
$$

The index exploits the contrast between chlorophyll absorption in the red band and leaf mesophyll scattering in the NIR band. Its value ranges from **−1 to +1**, with healthy dense vegetation typically showing values **> 0.5**, water bodies **< 0**, and bare soil around **0.1–0.2**. It is widely used in agriculture, drought monitoring, and biomass estimation.

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark formula + 1 mark application/significance)*

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between supervised and unsupervised classification.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Supervised | Unsupervised |
|---|---|---|
| Training data | Requires analyst-defined training samples | No training data required |
| Prior knowledge | Required to label classes | Not required |
| Algorithm examples | MLC, SVM, Minimum Distance, Random Forest | K-Means, ISODATA, Fuzzy C-Means |
| Output | Predefined thematic classes | Statistically-derived clusters (require labelling) |
| Accuracy | Generally higher if training is good | Lower, but useful for unknown landscapes |

*(Allocation: 1 mark definition + 1 mark key difference + 1 mark example)*

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the discriminant function for the Maximum Likelihood Classifier (MLC) and explain the role of each term. Discuss why it reduces to the Minimum Distance Classifier under specific conditions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a suitable sketch, describe the supervised classification workflow and explain how the accuracy of the resulting thematic map is assessed using a confusion matrix. Compute the Kappa coefficient for the following 3-class confusion matrix: **(7 marks)**

| Classified → | Forest | Water | Urban | Row Total |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Ref: Forest** | 45 | 3 | 2 | 50 |
| **Ref: Water** | 1 | 38 | 1 | 40 |
| **Ref: Urban** | 4 | 2 | 44 | 50 |
| **Col Total** | 50 | 43 | 47 | **N = 140** |

---

### **Model Solution A(a) – 7 marks:**

The Bayesian decision framework is:

$$
\text{Choose } \omega_c \text{ that maximizes } P(\omega_c \mid x) \propto P(x \mid \omega_c) \cdot P(\omega_c)
$$

For a multivariate Gaussian distribution in $n$-dimensional spectral space:

$$
P(x \mid \omega_c) = \frac{1}{(2\pi)^{n/2}\vert\Sigma_c\vert^{1/2}} \exp\left[-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_c)^T\Sigma_c^{-1}(x-\mu_c)\right]
$$

Taking the logarithm and discarding the constant term:

$$
g_c(x) = \ln P(\omega_c) - \frac{1}{2}\ln\vert\Sigma_c\vert - \frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_c)^T\Sigma_c^{-1}(x-\mu_c)
$$

**Role of each term:**
- **$\ln P(\omega_c)$** — Prior probability term; encodes the analyst's belief about the relative frequency of class $c$.
- **$-\frac{1}{2}\ln\vert\Sigma_c\vert$** — Variance penalty term; classes with large spread (large $\vert\Sigma_c\vert$) are penalized because they are less "specific."
- **$-\frac{1}{2}(x-\mu_c)^T\Sigma_c^{-1}(x-\mu_c)$** — Mahalanobis distance term; measures weighted spectral distance from pixel $x$ to class mean $\mu_c$.

**Reduction to Minimum Distance Classifier:**
When all classes have **equal covariance matrices**, i.e., $\Sigma_c = \sigma^2 I$, then $\ln\vert\Sigma_c\vert$ becomes equal for all classes and can be dropped. The prior term is also equal if classes are equiprobable. The discriminant simplifies to:

$$
g_c(x) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}(x-\mu_c)^T(x-\mu_c)
$$

Since $-\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}$ is constant, classification reduces to **minimizing Euclidean distance** $\vert x - \mu_c\vert$. This is the Minimum Distance Classifier.

*(Allocation: 2 marks for derivation, 3 marks for term-wise interpretation, 2 marks for special case reduction)*

---

### **Model Solution A(b) – 7 marks:**

**Confusion Matrix Computation:**

**Step 1 – Overall Accuracy (1 mark):**

$$
p_o = \frac{45 + 38 + 44}{140} = \frac{127}{140} = 0.9071 = 90.71\%
$$

**Step 2 – Marginal Products (2 marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Forest: } & 50 \times 50 = 2500 \\
\text{Water: } & 40 \times 43 = 1720 \\
\text{Urban: } & 50 \times 47 = 2350 \\
\text{Sum } \sum x_{i+} x_{+i} & = 6570
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 – Expected Chance Agreement (1 mark):**

$$
p_c = \frac{6570}{140^2} = \frac{6570}{19600} = 0.3352
$$

**Step 4 – Kappa Coefficient (2 marks):**

$$
\hat{K} = \frac{p_o - p_c}{1 - p_c} = \frac{0.9071 - 0.3352}{1 - 0.3352} = \frac{0.5719}{0.6648} = 0.8603
$$

**Step 5 – Interpretation (1 mark):**

$\hat{K} = 0.86$ falls in the **0.81 – 1.00** range, indicating **almost perfect agreement** between classified and reference data (Landis & Koch, 1977).

*(Allocation: 1 mark for overall accuracy, 2 marks for marginal products, 1 mark for $p_c$, 2 marks for $\hat{K}$ value, 1 mark for interpretation)*

---

### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` *(Alternative Choice)*

**(a)** Explain the concept of Principal Component Analysis (PCA) in remote sensing. Derive the eigen-equation used for computing principal components and discuss why PCA is useful as a feature-reduction technique for hyperspectral data. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare image differencing, image ratioing, and post-classification comparison as change detection techniques. With reference to NDVI differencing, calculate the change magnitude for a pixel whose NDVI values in 2020 and 2024 are **0.65** and **0.42** respectively, and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution B(a) – 7 marks:**

PCA is an orthogonal linear transformation that converts correlated multispectral bands into **uncorrelated principal components (PCs)** ordered by decreasing variance. The first PC captures the maximum variance, the second PC captures the next highest variance orthogonal to PC1, and so on.

**Derivation of the Eigen-equation (5 marks):**

Given an $n$-band image with data matrix $X$ (rows = pixels, columns = bands), centered to have zero mean, the covariance matrix is:

$$
\Sigma = \frac{1}{N-1} X^T X
$$

We seek a linear transformation $Y = X W$ where $W$ is an orthonormal matrix such that the variance of $Y$ is maximized. The variance of the $k$-th PC is:

$$
\text{Var}(Y_k) = W_k^T \Sigma W_k
$$

To maximize this subject to $W_k^T W_k = 1$, we use the **Lagrange multiplier method**:

$$
\mathcal{L}(W_k, \lambda) = W_k^T \Sigma W_k - \lambda(W_k^T W_k - 1)
$$

Setting the gradient to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial W_k} = 2 \Sigma W_k - 2 \lambda W_k = 0
$$

This gives the **eigen-equation**:

$$
\boxed{\Sigma W_k = \lambda W_k}
$$

Thus the principal component directions are the **eigenvectors** of $\Sigma$, and the variance captured by each PC equals the corresponding **eigenvalue** $\lambda_k$.

**Why PCA for Hyperspectral Data (2 marks):**
- Reduces dimensionality (e.g., from **200+ hyperspectral bands** to **3–5 PCs**) capturing >95% variance.
- Removes inter-band correlation.
- Suppresses noise (concentrated in lower-order PCs).
- Improves classification accuracy and computational efficiency.

---

### **Model Solution B(b) – 7 marks:**

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Method | Formula | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Image Differencing** | $D = X_2 - X_1$ | Simple, detects intensity changes | Sensitive to radiometric noise; no directional info |
| **Image Ratioing** | $R = X_2 / X_1$ | Reduces illumination effects | Non-Gaussian distribution; undefined when $X_1 = 0$ |
| **Post-Classification Comparison** | Compare classified maps of two dates | Provides class-to-class transitions | Errors compound from both classifications |

**NDVI Differencing Calculation (3 marks):**

$$
\Delta \text{NDVI} = \text{NDVI}_{2024} - \text{NDVI}_{2020} = 0.42 - 0.65 = -0.23
$$

**Interpretation (1 mark):**

A **negative change of 0.23** indicates a **decrease in vegetation vigour** between 2020 and 2024. This may signify deforestation, drought stress, urbanization, crop harvesting, or disease infestation. The magnitude is **moderate-to-high** (typical thresholds: $\vert\Delta\text{NDVI}\vert > 0.2$ = significant change).

*(Allocation: 3 marks for comparison, 3 marks for calculation, 1 mark for interpretation)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Sign error in NDVI**: Always NIR − Red on the numerator; never Red − NIR.
> - **Confusing Producer's vs User's Accuracy**: Producer's uses **row totals** (omission), User's uses **column totals** (commission). Examiners deduct **1 mark** for this swap.
> - **Kappa without expected agreement**: Many students compute overall accuracy only and call it accuracy assessment. Always compute $\hat{K}$ explicitly — losing **2 marks** otherwise.
> - **PCA eigen-equation**: Writing $\Sigma W = W \Lambda$ is acceptable but writing it as $\Sigma W = \lambda W$ and identifying $W$ as eigenvectors and $\lambda$ as eigenvalues is the **expected** form.
> - **NDVI range**: Forgetting that NDVI is bounded in $[-1, +1]$ and reporting values outside this range will attract a **1-mark penalty**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **NDVI** formula: $\text{NDVI} = \frac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$; range $[-1, +1]$.
- **NDWI** detects water: $\text{NDWI} = \frac{\text{Green} - \text{NIR}}{\text{Green} + \text{NIR}}$.
- **NDBI** detects built-up: $\text{NDBI} = \frac{\text{SWIR} - \text{NIR}}{\text{SWIR} + \text{NIR}}$.
- **EVI** is atmospheric-resistant; preferred over NDVI in tropical regions.
- **SAVI** uses $L = 0.5$ for sparse canopy correction.
- **Maximum Likelihood** uses **Gaussian assumption** + Bayesian framework; it is the most accurate among classical classifiers but computationally expensive.
- **Minimum Distance** assumes **equal covariance** (spherical clusters) — fastest but least accurate.
- **Parallelepiped** uses **min-max bounding boxes** — fast but prone to unclassified pixels when boxes do not cover spectral space.
- **Overall Accuracy** = trace of confusion matrix / total samples.
- **Producer's Accuracy** = column-based; related to **omission errors**.
- **User's Accuracy** = row-based; related to **commission errors**.
- **Kappa** = $\frac{N\sum x_{ii} - \sum(x_{i+}x_{+i})}{N^2 - \sum(x_{i+}x_{+i})}$; thresholds: 0.0 / 0.20 / 0.40 / 0.60 / 0.80 / 1.00.
- **PCA** uses eigen-equation $\Sigma v = \lambda v$; first PC = maximum variance direction.
- **Change Detection** methods: Differencing (simple, noisy), Ratioing (illumination-invariant), Post-Classification (class-to-class).
- **Change Vector** has both **magnitude** $\vert\Delta\vert$ and **direction** (angle in spectral space).
- **Hyperspectral** data (200+ bands) requires dimensionality reduction via PCA or MNF (Minimum Noise Fraction).
- **Landsat-8**: 30 m multispectral, 16-day revisit, 12-bit radiometric.
- **Sentinel-2**: 10–20 m, 5-day revisit, 13 spectral bands.
- **MODIS**: 250–1000 m, daily revisit — best for temporal change analysis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT863 - REMOTE SENSING AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Software # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Definition of Remote Sensing & Control Software **Remote Sensing Software** refers to the integrated suite of computer programs, algorithms, libraries, and graphical interfaces engineered to acquire, ingest, process, analyze, visualize, and interpret geospatial data captured by airborne and spaceborne sensors. In the context of a unified *Remote Sensing and Control* discipline, this software layer also extends to **control system design platforms** that simulate, model, and validate closed-loop control algorithms applied to unmanned aerial vehicles (UAVs), satellites, robotic rovers, and ground-based sensor-actuator networks. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Remote sensing software is the computational bridge that converts raw, uncalibrated electromagnetic radiance values (digital numbers – DNs) into physically meaningful georeferenced thematic products (e.g., land cover maps, vegetation indices, digital elevation models) and provides the graphical user interface (GUI) for the operator to execute control commands. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Analogy — The Digital Darkroom and Cockpit Fusion:** > Imagine the entire Remote Sensing workflow as a **photographer-pilot hybrid mission**. The satellite (or drone) is the *eye-in-the-sky*, capturing the photograph. **Software is simultaneously the darkroom AND the cockpit**: > - As a **darkroom**, it develops the raw "film" (DN values) into a sharpened, color-balanced, geo-tagged picture using chemical-like *algorithms* (radiometric calibration, atmospheric correction, geometric registration). > - As a **cockpit**, it hosts the *control dashboard* that tells the sensor where to look next (gimbal pointing, scan angle programming), adjusts gain settings (automatic gain control – AGC), and manages onboard memory buffers before downlink. > > Without this digital darkroom-cockpit, the sensor's data is just an unintelligible stream of numbers — like receiving an unexposed roll of film with no chemistry to develop it. ## 1.3 Major Categories of Remote Sensing & Control Software | Category | Primary Role | KTU Exam-Relevant Examples | |---|---|---| | **Image Processing & GIS Suites** | End-to-end raster/vector analysis | ERDAS IMAGINE, ENVI, ArcGIS Pro, QGIS | | **Open-Source Scientific Platforms** | Free academic/research workflows | QGIS, SNAP (Sentinel Application Platform), Google Earth Engine (GEE), GRASS GIS | | **Numerical Computing & Control Design** | Algorithm prototyping, control simulation | MATLAB, Simulink, SciPy/NumPy, Octave | | **UAV Mission Planning & Control** | Flight path generation, telemetry | Mission Planner, QGroundControl, Pix4D | | **Cloud-Based Geospatial Platforms** | Petabyte-scale parallel processing | Google Earth Engine, AWS Open Data, Microsoft Planetary Computer | | **Specialized Toolboxes** | Hyperspectral, SAR, LiDAR | Opticks, PolSARpro, LAStools, CloudCompare | ## 1.4 KTU Key Takeaway > [!IMPORTANT] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (bolded for recall):** > - Standard **radiometric resolution**: **8-bit (256 DNs), 11-bit, 12-bit, 16-bit**. > - Standard **spatial resolution tiers**: Very High (**<1 m**), High (**1–10 m**), Medium (**10–100 m**), Low (**>100 m**). > - **GDAL (Geospatial Data Abstraction Library)** is the de facto open-source translator supporting **>200 raster and vector formats**. > - **WGS-84 datum** is the global horizontal reference for almost all satellite-derived products. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Layered Software Architecture of a Remote Sensing Workflow A modern remote sensing software stack follows a **five-tier layered architecture**, mirroring the OSI-style abstraction common in control engineering. Each tier can be examined independently for KTU questions. **Tier 1 — Data Acquisition & Driver Layer** - Handles raw ingestion of Level-0 (L0) products from sensor downlink, USB, FTP, or cloud buckets. - Implements format demultiplexers (e.g., HDF5, GeoTIFF, NetCDF, SAFE, DIMAP). - Calls driver libraries such as **GDAL/OGR** to expose data to higher tiers. **Tier 2 — Pre-Processing Layer (Radiometric & Geometric Correction)** - Converts raw DN → Top-of-Atmosphere Radiance (L) → Top-of-Atmosphere Reflectance (ρ*) → Surface Reflectance (ρ_s). - Performs orthorectification, atmospheric correction (e.g., **Dark Object Subtraction – DOS**, **6S model**, **FLAASH**, **Sen2Cor**). - Removes noise using Lee, Frost, or Gamma-MAP filters. **Tier 3 — Analysis & Enhancement Layer** - Computes **spectral indices** (NDVI, NDWI, NDBI, EVI, SAVI). - Applies **transforms** (PCA, Tasseled Cap, IHS → RGB). - Conducts **classification** (supervised: Maximum Likelihood, SVM, Random Forest; unsupervised: K-Means, ISODATA). **Tier 4 — Control & Automation Layer** - Hosts **Model-Driven Architectures (MDA)** using OGC standards (WMS, WFS, WCS, WPS). - Implements **Python/R scripting APIs** for batch processing. - Integrates with **control systems** via ROS (Robot Operating System) nodes, MQTT telemetry, or UDP/TCP socket bridges for hardware-in-the-loop (HIL) testing. **Tier 5 — Visualization & Decision Support Layer** - 2D/3D rendering with hill-shading, fly-through animations. - Dashboards with charts, time-series, and alerts (e.g., ArcGIS Dashboards, GEE Apps). ## 2.2 Why Each Tier Matters — The Engineering 'Why' > [!NOTE] > - **Why Tier 1?** Without robust drivers, no upstream tool — even an AI engine — can read the data. > - **Why Tier 2?** A radiometric error of 1 DN in Band 4 of Landsat-8 can misclassify thousands of hectares of vegetation. > - **Why Tier 3?** Classification accuracy directly drives crop-yield, flood-risk, and deforestation decisions. > - **Why Tier 4?** Enables **closed-loop automation** — e.g., a wildfire detection algorithm can autonomously task a higher-resolution sensor to slew toward the hotspot. > - **Why Tier 5?** Decision-makers (administrators, farmers, military) require human-readable products, not raw matrices. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Meaning | Units / Typical Value | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **DN → Radiance:** $L_\lambda = \text{gain} \cdot \text{DN} + \text{offset}$ | $L_\lambda$ = spectral radiance; DN = digital number | $W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot sr^{-1} \cdot \mu m^{-1}$ | | 2 | **Radiance → TOA Reflectance:** $\rho^* = \dfrac{\pi \cdot L_\lambda \cdot d^2}{ESUN_\lambda \cdot \cos\theta_s}$ | $d$ = Earth–Sun distance (AU); $ESUN$ = exo-atmospheric solar irradiance; $\theta_s$ = solar zenith angle | dimensionless (0–1) | | 3 | **NDVI:** $\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$ | NIR, Red = surface reflectance bands | dimensionless (-1 to +1) | | 4 | **EVI:** $\text{EVI} = G \cdot \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + C_1 \cdot \text{Red} - C_2 \cdot \text{Blue} + L}$ | $G = 2.5$, $C_1 = 6$, $C_2 = 7.5$, $L = 1$ | dimensionless | | 5 | **Atmospheric Correction (DOS):** $\rho_s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (L_\lambda - L_p)}{ESUN_\lambda \cdot \cos\theta_s \cdot \tau}$ | $L_p$ = path radiance; $\tau$ = atmospheric transmittance | dimensionless | | 6 | **Geometric Registration RMSE:** $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - X_i)^2 + (y_i - Y_i)^2}{n}}$ | $(x,y)$ = ground truth, $(X,Y)$ = transformed pixel | meters / pixels | | 7 | **SAR Backscatter (dB):** $\sigma^0_{\text{dB}} = 10 \log_{10}\sigma^0_{\text{lin}}$ | $\sigma^0$ = normalized radar cross-section | decibels (dB) | | 8 | **LiDAR Point Density:** $\rho = \dfrac{N}{A}$ | $N$ = number of returns; $A$ = area | points / m² | | 9 | **Control Loop (PID — for sensor pointing):** $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t)\,dt + K_d \dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | $e(t)$ = pointing error | control signal (units vary) | | 10 | **Map Scale / Resolution:** $\text{GSD} = \dfrac{H \cdot p}{f}$ | $H$ = altitude; $p$ = pixel pitch; $f$ = focal length | meters / pixel | > [!WARNING] > In the markdown table above, all absolute-value or set-notation symbols have been replaced with **\vert** or **\mid** LaTeX commands to **prevent markdown table syntax corruption**. Do not use the literal pipe `|` character inside table rows. ## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Agriculture:** NDVI/EVI software pipelines drive precision-fertilizer prescription maps. - **Disaster Management:** GEE-based flood inundation mapping updates within hours of SAR acquisition. - **Defense & Surveillance:** MATLAB/Simulink models simulate satellite attitude-control loops before launch. - **Urban Planning:** ArcGIS + LiDAR point clouds generate city-scale Digital Twins. - **Climate Science:** NASA’s GEE hosts **>40 PB** of Landsat/Sentinel archive for time-series analytics. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: DN → Top-of-Atmosphere Reflectance (ρ*) The KTU-expected derivation chain converts a raw 8-bit or 16-bit digital number recorded by a sensor into a physically meaningful reflectance value. Every step is mandatory; partial credit is awarded per line. ### Step 1 — Sensor Quantization to Radiance A push-broom or whisk-broom sensor records incoming photon flux as an analog voltage, which is then quantized by an Analog-to-Digital Converter (ADC). The metadata file (e.g., Landsat MTL.txt, Sentinel-2 L1C XML) supplies per-band **radiometric calibration coefficients** $a$ (multiplicative gain) and $b$ (additive offset, often zero for modern sensors): $$ L_\lambda = a \cdot \text{DN} + b $$ - **Explanation:** $a$ converts one count of DN into $W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot sr^{-1} \cdot \mu m^{-1}$. For Landsat-8 OLI Band 4 (Red), $a \approx 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$ and $b \approx 0$. ### Step 2 — Correction for Earth–Sun Distance Variation The Earth orbits the Sun in an ellipse, so $d$ (Earth–Sun distance in Astronomical Units) varies between **0.983 AU (perihelion)** and **1.017 AU (aphelion)**. The radiance measured at the satellite corresponds to a different Sun–Earth distance on different dates. To normalize, we apply the inverse-square law: $$ \rho^* = \dfrac{\pi \cdot L_\lambda \cdot d^2}{ESUN_\lambda \cdot \cos\theta_s} $$ ### Step 3 — Cosine Correction for Solar Zenith Angle At high latitudes or winter, $\theta_s$ (solar zenith angle) approaches 90°, making $\cos\theta_s \rightarrow 0$ and reflectance blow up. The cosine term **projects** the incoming irradiance onto the horizontal ground surface: $$ \cos\theta_s = \sin(\phi) \cdot \sin(\delta) + \cos(\phi) \cdot \cos(\delta) \cdot \cos(h) $$ where $\phi$ = latitude, $\delta$ = solar declination, $h$ = hour angle. ### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU Style) > **Given (typical KTU problem):** > A Landsat-8 OLI pixel records DN = 12000 in Band 4 (Red). Metadata gives $a = 2.0 \times 10^{-3}$, $b = 0$, $ESUN = 1500 \, W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot \mu m^{-1}$, $d = 1.02 \, \text{AU}$, $\theta_s = 30^\circ$. **Step-by-step evaluation:** $$ L_\lambda = (2.0 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 12000 + 0 = 24 \, W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot sr^{-1} \cdot \mu m^{-1} $$ $$ \cos(30^\circ) = 0.8660 $$ $$ \rho^* = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 24 \cdot (1.02)^2}{1500 \cdot 0.8660} = \dfrac{3.1416 \cdot 24 \cdot 1.0404}{1299} $$ $$ \rho^* = \dfrac{78.42}{1299} \approx 0.0604 $$ **Interpretation:** A TOA reflectance of **0.0604 (6.04 %)** in the Red band is consistent with a moderately vegetated or bare-soil pixel — perfectly reasonable. ## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: NDVI Computation & Classification Threshold $$ \text{NDVI} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}} $$ Assuming NIR = 0.45 and Red = 0.06 (consistent with our worked example), the evaluation proceeds: $$ \text{NDVI} = \dfrac{0.45 - 0.06}{0.45 + 0.06} = \dfrac{0.39}{0.51} \approx 0.7647 $$ **Interpretation band (typical KTU thresholds):** | NDVI Range | Land Cover Class | |---|---| | < 0.0 | Water, Snow, Clouds | | 0.0 – 0.2 | Bare Soil, Rock, Built-up | | 0.2 – 0.4 | Sparse Vegetation, Dry Crops | | 0.4 – 0.6 | Moderate Vegetation, Grassland | | > 0.6 | Dense Vegetation, Forest | ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Software Selection for KTU Use Cases | Application | Required Algorithm | Recommended Software | Open-Source Alternative | |---|---|---|---| | Land cover mapping | Supervised classification | ENVI + ROI tools | QGIS + Orfeo ToolBox, SCP plugin | | Time-series vegetation monitoring | NDVI/EVI stack | Google Earth Engine | R + `terra` package | | Hyperspectral mineral mapping | Spectral Angle Mapper (SAM) | ENVI | Python + `spectral` library | | SAR flood mapping | Threshold + change detection | SNAP (ESA) | ESA SNAP (free) | | Control system of UAV | PID tuning | MATLAB/Simulink | Octave + `control` pkg | | 3D city model | LiDAR point cloud + photogrammetry | Pix4D, TerraScan | OpenDroneMap, CloudCompare | | Web map publication | OGC services | ArcGIS Enterprise | GeoServer, MapServer | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 End-to-End Remote Sensing Software Processing Pipeline The diagram below illustrates a typical Level-0 → Level-4 processing pipeline implemented in a software suite such as SNAP, GEE, or a Python stack built on top of **GDAL/Rasterio**. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[sensorL0
Raw Telemetry
DN Packets] --> B[driverStage
GDAL Driver Layer
Format Decode] B --> C[radCalibStage
Radiometric Calibration
DN to Radiance] C --> D[atmCorStage
Atmospheric Correction
DOS or 6S Model] D --> E[geoRegStage
Geometric Registration
Orthorectification] E --> F[analysisStage
Index and Transform
NDVI, PCA, Tasseled Cap] F --> G[classifyStage
Classification Engine
SVM, Random Forest] G --> H[controlStage
Control and Automation
PID, Scripting API] H --> I[vizStage
Visualization Layer
2D and 3D Dashboard] I --> J[decisionStage
Decision Support
End User Output] A --> A1[HDF5, GeoTIFF, SAFE] D --> D1[Surface Reflectance] G --> G1[Thematic Map] H --> H1[ROS Node, MQTT] classDef io fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000 classDef proc fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000 classDef out fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 class A,B,I,J io class C,D,E,F,G,H proc class D1,G1,H1 out ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Remote Sensing + Control Software Stack This block diagram (a Mermaid-safe fallback for complex physical schematics) shows how the *Software* topic interfaces with both the *Remote Sensing* and the *Control* halves of your KTU course code PEICT863. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph sensorBlock[PHYSICAL SENSOR LAYER] sat1[Earth Observation Satellite] uav1[UAV Multispectral Camera] sar1[SAR Payload] lidar1[LiDAR Scanner] end subgraph ingestBlock[SOFTWARE LAYER 1: INGESTION] driver1[GDAL and OGR Drivers] demux1[Format Demultiplexer] end subgraph preProcBlock[SOFTWARE LAYER 2: PRE PROCESSING] rad1[Radiometric Calibration] atm1[Atmospheric Correction] geo1[Geometric Correction] end subgraph anaBlock[SOFTWARE LAYER 3: ANALYSIS] idx1[Spectral Indices] cls1[Classification Engine] feat1[Feature Extraction] end subgraph ctrlBlock[SOFTWARE LAYER 4: CONTROL] sim1[MATLAB and Simulink] pid1[PID and MPC Loops] hl1[Hardware in the Loop] end subgraph outBlock[SOFTWARE LAYER 5: OUTPUT] gis1[GIS Dashboard] alert1[Alert and Tasking API] arch1[Cloud Archive] end sat1 --> driver1 uav1 --> driver1 sar1 --> driver1 lidar1 --> driver1 driver1 --> demux1 demux1 --> rad1 rad1 --> atm1 atm1 --> geo1 geo1 --> idx1 geo1 --> cls1 geo1 --> feat1 idx1 --> sim1 cls1 --> sim1 feat1 --> pid1 sim1 --> pid1 pid1 --> hl1 hl1 --> alert1 cls1 --> gis1 idx1 --> arch1 alert1 --> sat1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the feedback arrow:** The bottom edge `alert1 --> sat1` represents the **closed-loop tasking** pathway — a software-detected anomaly (e.g., wildfire) automatically commands the satellite to slew its sensor toward the hotspot on the next pass. This is the unifying theme of *Remote Sensing **and Control*** in your course code. ## 4.3 Diagram Interpretation Cheat-Sheet for KTU | Block | Software Responsible | KTU Keyword | |---|---|---| | Ingestion | GDAL, NetCDF, HDF5 libraries | "Driver Layer" | | Pre-Processing | ENVI, SNAP, Sen2Cor | "Calibration & Correction" | | Analysis | GEE, ArcGIS, Orfeo ToolBox | "Information Extraction" | | Control | MATLAB/Simulink, ROS, LabVIEW | "Closed-Loop Control" | | Output | GeoServer, ArcGIS Dashboards | "Decision Support" | # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **Q:** Define *remote sensing software*. List any **four** major commercial and **four** open-source packages used in remote sensing and control applications. **Model Answer:** Remote sensing software is a collection of computer programs and algorithms designed to acquire, process, analyze, and visualize geospatial data acquired by airborne or spaceborne sensors, and to model/control the associated sensor platforms. **Commercial (any 4):** ERDAS IMAGINE, ENVI, ArcGIS Pro, MATLAB, ER Mapper, PCI Geomatica, eCognition. **Open-Source (any 4):** QGIS, GRASS GIS, SNAP, Google Earth Engine, GDAL, Orfeo ToolBox, SAGA GIS, R (`terra`/`raster`). ### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Q:** What is the role of **GDAL** in a remote sensing software stack? Mention its key capabilities. **Model Answer:** GDAL (Geospatial Data Abstraction Library) is an open-source translator library that provides a single abstract data model for reading and writing **>200 raster and vector geospatial formats** (GeoTIFF, HDF5, NetCDF, JPEG2000, Shapefile, GPKG, KML, etc.). It acts as the universal **driver layer** between raw sensor files and higher-level software such as QGIS, ArcGIS, Google Earth Engine, and Python environments (via `rasterio`, `fiona`). It also provides built-in utilities for reprojection, resampling, warping, and DEM processing. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module-Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries **14 marks** and contains **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. Students answer *either* Question A *or* Question B. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **A(a) [7 Marks]:** Explain in detail the **layered architecture** of a typical remote sensing software stack. With the aid of a block diagram, describe each layer's role from data acquisition to decision support. **Model Solution:** A modern remote sensing software stack is organized into five logical layers: 1. **Data Acquisition Layer:** Manages ingestion of Level-0 products from sensors. Uses GDAL/OGR drivers to decode formats (HDF5, SAFE, GeoTIFF). Handles FTP/Cloud-bucket downloading. 2. **Pre-Processing Layer:** Performs radiometric calibration (DN → radiance → reflectance), atmospheric correction (DOS, 6S, FLAASH), and geometric orthorectification using a DEM and ground-control points. 3. **Analysis Layer:** Computes spectral indices (NDVI, NDWI, EVI), executes image transformations (PCA, Tasseled Cap, IHS), and runs classification algorithms (Maximum Likelihood, SVM, Random Forest). 4. **Control & Automation Layer:** Provides scripting APIs (Python, R), hosts control models in MATLAB/Simulink, and implements tasking protocols for sensor slewing and PID-based pointing control. 5. **Visualization & Decision Layer:** Renders 2D/3D maps, publishes OGC web services, and feeds dashboards to end users. > [Draw the **Block Diagram from SECTION 4.2** above — examiners award 2 marks for the diagram itself.] **A(b) [7 Marks]:** A Landsat-8 OLI sensor records DN = 9500 in Band 5 (NIR, 0.86 µm). Given $a = 0.97 \times 10^{-2}$, $b = -0.29$, $ESUN = 972 \, W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot \mu m^{-1}$, $d = 0.99$ AU, $\theta_s = 45^\circ$. Compute the **TOA reflectance** and the **NDVI** using $ρ^*_{\text{Red}} = 0.0604$. **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Radiance:** $$ L_\lambda = (0.97 \times 10^{-2}) \cdot 9500 + (-0.29) = 92.15 - 0.29 = 91.86 \, W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot sr^{-1} \cdot \mu m^{-1} $$ **Step 2 — TOA Reflectance in NIR:** $$ \rho^*_{\text{NIR}} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 91.86 \cdot (0.99)^2}{972 \cdot \cos(45^\circ)} = \dfrac{3.1416 \cdot 91.86 \cdot 0.9801}{972 \cdot 0.7071} $$ $$ \rho^*_{\text{NIR}} = \dfrac{282.86}{687.30} \approx 0.4116 $$ **Step 3 — NDVI (with given Red = 0.0604):** $$ \text{NDVI} = \dfrac{0.4116 - 0.0604}{0.4116 + 0.0604} = \dfrac{0.3512}{0.4720} \approx 0.7441 $$ **Interpretation:** NDVI = 0.7441 ⇒ **dense vegetation / forest cover**. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **B(a) [7 Marks]:** Compare **commercial, open-source, and cloud-based** remote sensing software platforms. Give two representative examples of each and state **two advantages and one limitation** per category. **Model Solution:** | Category | Examples | Advantages | Limitation | |---|---|---|---| | **Commercial** | ENVI, ERDAS IMAGINE, ArcGIS Pro | Industry-standard support; certified algorithms; GUI ergonomics | High license cost (₹ lakhs); closed source | | **Open-Source Desktop** | QGIS, GRASS GIS, SNAP | Free, auditable code; vibrant community | Steeper learning curve; fragmented documentation | | **Cloud-Based** | Google Earth Engine, Microsoft Planetary Computer | Petabyte-scale parallel compute; no local install | Requires stable internet; quota limits on free tier | | **Numerical/Control** | MATLAB/Simulink, Octave | Industry standard for control design | License cost; vendor lock-in | > [Include a tabular comparison — examiners award 1 mark for structure.] **B(b) [7 Marks]:** Describe the workflow of **atmospheric correction** using the **Dark Object Subtraction (DOS)** method in software. State the formula and identify which atmospheric effect it primarily removes. **Model Solution:** **Workflow Steps:** 1. **Histogram Inspection:** Open the radiance image band in software (e.g., QGIS raster histogram). Identify the **Dark Object** — the darkest pixel cluster (deep clear-water body or dense shadow). 2. **Path Radiance Estimation:** Record the minimum DN or radiance value, $L_p$, of the dark object. This represents the **additive path radiance** scattered into the sensor's line of sight. 3. **Subtraction:** Subtract $L_p$ from every pixel: $$ L_{\text{corrected}} = L_\lambda - L_p $$ 4. **Cosine & ESUN Normalization:** Convert $L_{\text{corrected}}$ to surface reflectance using the standard formula: $$ \rho_s = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (L_\lambda - L_p)}{ESUN_\lambda \cdot \cos\theta_s \cdot \tau} $$ 5. **Iterative Refinement:** Re-inspect the histogram; if the dark-object DN has shifted, repeat until convergence. **Primary Effect Removed:** **Additive path radiance** caused by **Rayleigh and aerosol scattering** in the atmosphere. > [The examiner expects 7 marks distributed as: 2 marks for workflow, 3 marks for formulas, 1 mark for effect identification, 1 mark for diagram/sketch if any.] --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps:** > 1. **Units Confusion:** Many students forget to convert $\theta_s$ from degrees to radians before applying cosine. Always write $\cos(\theta_s \cdot \pi/180)$ in software code. > 2. **Pipes in Markdown Tables:** If you transcribe `|x|` into a markdown formula sheet, it will break the table. Always use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert`. > 3. **ESUN vs. $E_{\text{sun}}$:** Both notations appear in metadata. They are the **same quantity** — the mean exo-atmospheric solar spectral irradiance. > 4. **NDVI ≠ Classification:** NDVI alone is **not** a substitute for a full supervised classification. State this caveat explicitly in 1-mark short-answer questions. > 5. **GDAL vs. OGR:** GDAL = raster, OGR = vector. Confusing these loses half a mark on 3-mark Part-A questions. > 6. **Cloud ≠ Local:** Do **not** claim Google Earth Engine "runs locally" — it is server-side parallel processing. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Software":** - **Definition:** Software = bridge between raw sensor data and meaningful geo-information *plus* control of the sensor platform. - **Five-tier architecture:** Ingestion → Pre-processing → Analysis → Control/Automation → Visualization/Output. - **GDAL** = universal raster/vector driver; supports >200 formats. - **Key Formula #1:** $L_\lambda = a \cdot \text{DN} + b$ (DN → Radiance). - **Key Formula #2:** $\rho^* = \dfrac{\pi L_\lambda d^2}{ESUN_\lambda \cos\theta_s}$ (Radiance → TOA Reflectance). - **Key Formula #3:** $\text{NDVI} = \dfrac{\text{NIR} - \text{Red}}{\text{NIR} + \text{Red}}$. - **Key Formula #4:** $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{\sum (x_i - X_i)^2 + (y_i - Y_i)^2}{n}}$ (geometric accuracy). - **Cloud Platforms:** Google Earth Engine, Microsoft Planetary Computer, AWS Open Data. - **Control Integration:** MATLAB/Simulink for PID tuning; ROS for robot/sensor integration; MQTT/UDP for telemetry. - **Open-source giants:** QGIS, SNAP, GRASS, GEE, Orfeo ToolBox, GDAL, R `terra`. - **Commercial giants:** ArcGIS Pro, ENVI, ERDAS IMAGINE, MATLAB, Pix4D. - **Atmospheric Correction Methods:** DOS, 6S, FLAASH, Sen2Cor, LaSRC. - **Classification Algorithms:** Maximum Likelihood, SVM, Random Forest, K-Means, ISODATA, Deep CNN. - **Standard Thresholds:** NDVI > 0.6 ⇒ dense vegetation; RMSE < 0.5 pixel ⇒ acceptable geometric registration. - **Closed-loop Tasking:** Software-detected anomaly → satellite re-tasking → next-pass high-res acquisition. - **PID for Sensor Pointing:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e\,dt + K_d \dot e$. - **Remember to draw the block diagram** in 14-mark answers — it is worth **2–3 marks** even before text. --- > **End of Notes — Module 4 / Topic: Software | PEICT863 | KTU 2024 Scheme**

topics.title: Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEICT863 - REMOTE SENSING AND CONTROL > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Applications ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Remote Sensing Applications** refer to the systematic deployment of electromagnetic radiation-based observation technologies (passive and active sensors mounted on airborne or spaceborne platforms) to acquire, interpret, and analyze physical, chemical, and biological characteristics of targets on the Earth's surface, atmosphere, and oceans without making physical contact. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for PEICT863, Module 4 consolidates the theoretical knowledge from Modules 1–3 into domain-specific applications spanning agriculture, urban planning, disaster management, hydrology, forestry, oceanography, and atmospheric sciences. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** Module 4 of PEICT863 is **purely application-oriented** and frequently appears in KTU examinations as 14-mark descriptive questions asking students to explain specific domain applications, sensor choices, and image processing workflows. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a doctor who diagnoses a patient **without touching them** — by analyzing body temperature (thermal imaging), blood oxygenation (multispectral analysis), and bone structure (radar penetration). Remote sensing works exactly the same way for Planet Earth. - **The Sun / Radar source** = the doctor's light/lamp - **The Earth's surface** = the patient - **The satellite sensor** = the doctor's diagnostic instruments - **The analyst's computer** = the doctor's brain interpreting X-rays Just as a doctor chooses different diagnostic tools for different ailments, a remote sensing scientist chooses **specific wavelength bands, sensors (e.g., LISS-III, OCM, AWSAR), and platforms (LEO, GEO)** for each application. For example, detecting **chlorophyll in crops** requires near-infrared (NIR) sensitivity, while detecting **flooded regions** requires synthetic aperture radar (SAR) that penetrates clouds and rain. > [!NOTE] > **Key Standard Metrics in Remote Sensing Applications:** > - **Spatial Resolution:** typically **10 m** (Sentinel-2), **30 m** (Landsat-8 OLI), **0.3 m** (WorldView-3) > - **Temporal Resolution (Revisit):** **5 days** (Sentinel-2), **16 days** (Landsat-8) > - **Radiometric Resolution:** **8-bit** to **16-bit** quantization > - **Spectral Range Utilized:** **0.4 µm to 14 µm** (VIS–SWIR–TIR) ### 1.3 Classification of Remote Sensing Applications The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes applications into the following high-yield domains: | Domain | Primary Sensor Type | Key Output Product | |---|---|---| | Agriculture & Crop Monitoring | Multispectral (VIS-NIR-SWIR) | NDVI, crop yield maps | | Urban Planning & LULC | High-resolution optical + SAR | Land Use / Land Cover maps | | Water Resources & Hydrology | SAR (C-band, L-band) | Flood inundation, watershed maps | | Disaster Management | SAR + Thermal | Damage assessment maps | | Forestry | Multispectral + LiDAR | Canopy density, biomass | | Oceanography | Ocean colour monitors | Chlorophyll-a, SST | | Atmosphere & Climate | Hyperspectral sounders | AOD, CO₂, ozone profiles | | Military & Defence | SAR + Hyperspectral | Target detection, terrain analysis | | Geology & Mineral Exploration | Hyperspectral SWIR | Mineral alteration maps | | Snow & Glacier Studies | SAR + Multispectral | Snow cover, glacier mass balance | ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Remote Sensing Applications Every remote sensing application follows a **standardized workflow**. Understanding this pipeline is critical for KTU exam answers. **Step 1: Mission Design & Sensor Selection** - The application dictates the required **spatial, spectral, radiometric, and temporal resolution**. - Example: Monitoring **cyclone dynamics** requires **high temporal resolution (≤3 hr)**, achievable only from **Geostationary platforms (e.g., INSAT-3DR)**. **Step 2: Data Acquisition** - Passive sensors record **solar-reflected or thermally-emitted radiance**. - Active sensors (e.g., **SAR, LiDAR**) transmit their own pulse and record backscatter. **Step 3: Pre-processing** - **Radiometric Correction:** Converts DN to radiance/reflectance using calibration coefficients. - **Atmospheric Correction:** Removes path radiance and gaseous absorption (e.g., using **Dark Object Subtraction (DOS)** or **FLAASH** model). - **Geometric Correction:** Orthorectification using **Ground Control Points (GCPs)** and a **Digital Elevation Model (DEM)**. **Step 4: Image Enhancement & Transformation** - Band ratioing, principal component analysis (PCA), texture analysis, and IHS sharpening. **Step 5: Information Extraction** - **Visual interpretation** using elements such as tone, texture, pattern, shape, size, shadow, association, and site. - **Digital classification** using supervised (e.g., **Maximum Likelihood, SVM, Random Forest**) and unsupervised (e.g., **K-Means, ISODATA**) algorithms. **Step 6: Accuracy Assessment** - Confusion matrix → **Overall Accuracy, Kappa Coefficient, Producer's & User's Accuracy**. ### 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Step - **Why pre-process?** Raw satellite data contains sensor noise, atmospheric scattering, and geometric distortions that bias quantitative measurements. - **How does band ratioing help?** It normalizes illumination differences and suppresses topographic shadow effects, enhancing subtle spectral differences. - **Why hybrid classification?** Combining object-based and pixel-based methods (e.g., **GEOBIA**) leverages both spectral and contextual (textural/shape) information. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table contains the **highest-yield formulas** for Module 4 problems in KTU 2024 ESE. All symbols are exam-standard and conform to Lillesand & Kiefer conventions. | # | Formula | Description | Typical Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $NDVI = \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{RED}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{RED}}$ | Normalized Difference Vegetation Index | Vegetation health, crop vigour | | 2 | $NDWI = \dfrac{\rho_{GREEN} - \rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{GREEN} + \rho_{NIR}}$ | Normalized Difference Water Index | Open water body detection | | 3 | $MNDWI = \dfrac{\rho_{GREEN} - \rho_{SWIR}}{\rho_{GREEN} + \rho_{SWIR}}$ | Modified NDWI (Xu, 2006) | Urban water feature enhancement | | 4 | $NDBI = \dfrac{\rho_{SWIR} - \rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{SWIR} + \rho_{NIR}}$ | Normalized Difference Built-up Index | Urban area extraction | | 5 | $NBR = \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{SWIR2}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{SWIR2}}$ | Normalized Burn Ratio | Post-fire severity mapping | | 6 | $dNBR = NBR_{pre} - NBR_{post}$ | Differenced NBR | Burn severity classification | | 7 | $EVI = G \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{RED}}{\rho_{NIR} + C_1 \rho_{RED} - C_2 \rho_{BLUE} + L}$ | Enhanced Vegetation Index | Dense canopy, atmospheric correction | | 8 | $L_{\lambda} = \dfrac{L_{max\lambda} - L_{min\lambda}}{QCAL_{max} - QCAL_{min}} \cdot (DN - QCAL_{min}) + L_{min\lambda}$ | DN to Radiance conversion | Radiometric calibration | | 9 | $\rho = \dfrac{\pi \cdot L_{\lambda} \cdot d^2}{ESUN_{\lambda} \cdot \cos\theta_s}$ | Reflectance from radiance (Lambertian) | Surface reflectance retrieval | | 10 | $\sigma^0 = 10 \cdot \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{P_r}{P_t} \cdot \dfrac{(4\pi)^3 R^4}{G^2 \lambda^2 A} \right)$ | Radar backscatter coefficient (dB) | SAR applications | | 11 | $T_{brightness} = \dfrac{T_6}{\varepsilon}$ | Brightness temperature correction | Thermal emissivity separation | | 12 | $Kappa = \dfrac{N \sum x_{ii} - \sum (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i})}{N^2 - \sum (x_{i+} \cdot x_{+i})}$ | Kappa coefficient of agreement | Classification accuracy | | 13 | $OA = \dfrac{\text{correctly classified pixels}}{\text{total pixels}}$ | Overall Accuracy | Classification performance | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Values (KEEP MEMORIZED):** > - **NDVI range:** $-1$ to $+1$ (Healthy vegetation: $\geq 0.3$; Dense canopy: $\geq 0.6$) > - **EVI Coefficients:** $G = 2.5$, $C_1 = 6$, $C_2 = 7.5$, $L = 1$ > - **Kappa interpretation:** $\geq 0.80$ = Excellent; $0.60\text{–}0.80$ = Good; $< 0.40$ = Poor ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Smart City Planning (e.g., AMRUT, Smart Cities Mission):** NDBI + NDVI + NDWI composite maps drive **land use zoning**. - **Precision Agriculture (PMFBY crop insurance):** NDVI time-series feed into **yield estimation models**. - **Disaster Response (ISRO's Bhuvan Platform):** NBR + dNBR drive **real-time fire damage mapping**. - **Water Security (Jal Jeevan Mission):** SAR-derived surface water maps inform **rural drinking water supply**. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Deriving NDVI from First Principles of Reflectance The NDVI is the most frequently tested index in KTU examinations. Below is its full derivation. **Step 1 — Start with raw Top-of-Atmosphere (TOA) radiance recorded at the sensor:** $$ L_{\lambda}^{TOA} = L_{\lambda}^{path} + T_{\lambda} \cdot \rho_{\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{E_{\lambda}^{sun} \cdot \cos\theta_s}{\pi} + T_{\lambda} \cdot L_{\lambda}^{upwelling} $$ Where: - $L_{\lambda}^{path}$ = path radiance (Rayleigh + aerosol scattering) - $T_{\lambda}$ = atmospheric transmittance - $\rho_{\lambda}$ = surface reflectance - $E_{\lambda}^{sun}$ = exoatmospheric solar irradiance - $\theta_s$ = solar zenith angle **Step 2 — After atmospheric correction, isolate $\rho_{\lambda}$ for each band:** $$ \rho_{RED} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot L_{RED} \cdot d^2}{ESUN_{RED} \cdot \cos\theta_s} $$ $$ \rho_{NIR} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot L_{NIR} \cdot d^2}{ESUN_{NIR} \cdot \cos\theta_s} $$ **Step 3 — Form the difference (numerator) to enhance contrast between vegetation and soil:** $$ \Delta\rho = \rho_{NIR} - \rho_{RED} $$ **Logical reasoning:** Chlorophyll absorbs strongly in RED (high absorption) and the leaf mesophyll scatters strongly in NIR (high reflectance), creating a **large positive difference** for healthy vegetation. **Step 4 — Form the sum (denominator) to normalize for varying illumination:** $$ \Sigma\rho = \rho_{NIR} + \rho_{RED} $$ **Logical reasoning:** The sum normalizes out differences in solar angle, sensor gain, and atmospheric path radiance that affect both bands similarly. **Step 5 — Form the ratio to obtain a unitless, normalized index:** $$ NDVI = \dfrac{\Delta\rho}{\Sigma\rho} = \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{RED}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{RED}} $$ **Step 6 — Verify the boundary conditions:** - For **dense green vegetation:** $\rho_{NIR} \gg \rho_{RED} \Rightarrow NDVI \to +1$ - For **bare soil:** $\rho_{NIR} \approx \rho_{RED} \Rightarrow NDVI \to 0$ - For **water bodies:** $\rho_{RED} > \rho_{NIR} \Rightarrow NDVI \to -1$ - For **snow/ice:** $\rho_{RED} \approx \rho_{NIR} \Rightarrow NDVI \to 0$ ### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Sensor-to-Application Mapping > [!NOTE] > The matrix below is **directly aligned** with the KTU 2024 Module 4 learning outcome: *"Apply remote sensing techniques to real-world problem domains."* | Application | Recommended Sensor | Spectral Bands | Resolution | Index Used | Output Product | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | Crop Monitoring | Sentinel-2 MSI | 13 bands (VIS–SWIR) | 10–20 m | NDVI, EVI | Crop vigour map | | Drought Assessment | MODIS / Sentinel-1 | Red, NIR, C-band SAR | 250 m / 20 m | NDVI, VCI | Drought severity map | | Flood Mapping | Sentinel-1 Sentinel-5P | UV–VIS (340–675 nm) | 1–7 km | DOAS retrieval | AOD, NO₂, O₃ maps | ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (For 7-Mark Sub-Question) **Problem:** A pixel in a Landsat-8 OLI image has the following TOA reflectances (after atmospheric correction): $\rho_{RED} = 0.08$ (Band 4, 655 nm) and $\rho_{NIR} = 0.42$ (Band 5, 865 nm). Compute: (a) NDVI, (b) Classify the land cover, (c) If $\rho_{GREEN} = 0.10$ and $\rho_{SWIR1} = 0.22$, compute NDBI and NDBI − NDVI. **Solution:** **(a) NDVI calculation:** $$ NDVI = \dfrac{0.42 - 0.08}{0.42 + 0.08} = \dfrac{0.34}{0.50} = 0.68 $$ **(b) Land cover classification:** Since $NDVI = 0.68 \geq 0.6$, the pixel represents **dense, healthy vegetation** (likely mature crop or forest). **(c) NDBI calculation (using Band 6 = SWIR1 = 1.61 µm):** $$ NDBI = \dfrac{0.22 - 0.42}{0.22 + 0.42} = \dfrac{-0.20}{0.64} = -0.3125 $$ **Composite Built-up Index (CBI):** $$ CBI = NDBI - NDVI = -0.3125 - 0.68 = -0.9925 $$ **Interpretation:** A highly negative CBI confirms **non-built-up (vegetated)** land cover. This pixel is **definitely not urban**. > [!TIP] > **Exam Trick (KTU Board):** If asked *"Will this pixel be misclassified as built-up?"*, the answer is **NO** because $NDBI < 0$ and $NDVI > 0$, giving a strongly negative CBI — a robust indicator of vegetation dominance. ## 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **Define NDVI. Mention its significance in agriculture.** **Model Answer:** The **Normalized Difference Vegetation Index (NDVI)** is a dimensionless spectral index computed as: $$ NDVI = \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{RED}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{RED}} $$ It exploits the contrast between strong chlorophyll absorption in the **RED band (0.6–0.7 µm)** and high mesophyll reflectance in the **NIR band (0.7–1.3 µm)**. Its values range from **−1 to +1**, where: - $NDVI \geq 0.6$ → Dense, healthy vegetation - $0.2 \leq NDVI < 0.6$ → Moderate vegetation/crops - $NDVI < 0.2$ → Bare soil or sparse vegetation - $NDVI < 0$ → Water bodies **Significance in agriculture:** NDVI is used for **crop vigour mapping, acreage estimation, irrigation scheduling, drought assessment, and yield prediction** under schemes like PMFBY. *(3 marks)* --- **Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Why is SAR preferred over optical sensors for flood mapping?** **Model Answer:** **Synthetic Aperture Radar (SAR)** is preferred for flood mapping because: 1. **Cloud-penetrating capability** — operates in the microwave region (1–100 cm), unaffected by cloud cover (floods are often accompanied by monsoon clouds). 2. **All-weather, day-night operation** — independent of solar illumination. 3. **Specular reflection from water** — smooth water surfaces act as mirrors, producing very low backscatter (dark pixels in SAR), enabling **easy threshold-based water extraction**. 4. **C-band SAR (e.g., Sentinel-1, RISAT-1)** provides **10 m spatial resolution** sufficient for inundation mapping. 5. Optical sensors fail because clouds and rain obscure the flooded area. *(3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students commonly lose marks by (a) not stating **standardized formulas with proper notation**, (b) failing to **justify the choice of sensor and bands**, and (c) omitting **accuracy assessment** in classification workflows. Always include a **diagram** (block diagram or flowchart) — it adds **1–2 free marks** in KTU ESE valuation. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) — Agriculture + Urban Planning Theme** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **(a)** Explain in detail how remote sensing is used in **crop acreage and yield estimation**. Discuss the role of **NDVI, EVI, and vegetation-supervised classification** with suitable sensor recommendations. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Describe the use of **NDBI and the NDBI–NDVI composite** for urban sprawl monitoring. Illustrate with a worked example. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Crop Acreage and Yield Estimation using Remote Sensing (7 marks)** **Step 1 – Sensor Selection:** Multispectral sensors with **RED and NIR bands** are mandatory. Recommended sensors: **Sentinel-2 MSI (10–20 m, 5-day revisit)**, **Landsat-8 OLI (30 m, 16-day)**, **Resourcesat-2 LISS-III (24 m)**. **Step 2 – Crop Acreage Estimation:** - Acquire **multi-date cloud-free images** covering the cropping season (Kharif: June–Oct, Rabi: Nov–March). - Compute **NDVI time-series**: $$ NDVI_t = \dfrac{NIR_t - RED_t}{NIR_t + RED_t} $$ - Identify the **peak NDVI period** (when crops are in their maximum vegetative stage). - Apply **supervised classification** (e.g., **Maximum Likelihood**) with training samples collected from known crop fields. - Compute area of each class using: $$ A_{crop} = N_{pixels} \times A_{pixel} = N_{pixels} \times (GSD)^2 $$ where **GSD = Ground Sampling Distance** (e.g., 10 m for Sentinel-2). **Step 3 – Yield Estimation:** - Establish **empirical regression**: $$ Yield = a \cdot NDVI_{peak} + b $$ - Coefficients $a, b$ are calibrated using **ground-truth yield data** from CCE (Crop Cutting Experiments). - Advanced models use **EVI** (better in dense canopies) and machine learning (Random Forest, SVM) integrating **SST, soil moisture, and rainfall** as auxiliary inputs. **Step 4 – Accuracy Assessment:** Generate a **confusion matrix** and report **Overall Accuracy and Kappa Coefficient**. **Step 5 – Application:** Used by **Mahalanobis National Crop Forecast Centre (MNCFC)** for **FASAL project** and **Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY)**. --- **(b) NDBI for Urban Sprawl Monitoring (7 marks)** **Step 1 – Definition:** $$ NDBI = \dfrac{\rho_{SWIR1} - \rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{SWIR1} + \rho_{NIR}} $$ **Step 2 – Physical Basis:** Built-up surfaces (concrete, asphalt) have **higher SWIR reflectance** and **lower NIR reflectance** compared to vegetation. This contrast allows **NDBI to highlight urban pixels**. **Step 3 – Composite Built-up Index (CBI):** $$ CBI = \dfrac{NDBI - NDVI}{NDBI + NDVI} $$ A pixel is classified as **built-up** if $CBI > 0$, i.e., $NDBI > NDVI$. **Step 4 – Worked Example:** Given $\rho_{SWIR1} = 0.30$, $\rho_{NIR} = 0.22$, $\rho_{RED} = 0.18$: $$ NDBI = \dfrac{0.30 - 0.22}{0.30 + 0.22} = \dfrac{0.08}{0.52} = 0.1538 $$ $$ NDVI = \dfrac{0.22 - 0.18}{0.22 + 0.18} = \dfrac{0.04}{0.40} = 0.10 $$ $$ CBI = \dfrac{0.1538 - 0.10}{0.1538 + 0.10} = \dfrac{0.0538}{0.2538} = 0.212 $$ Since $CBI = 0.212 > 0$, the pixel is **classified as built-up/urban**. **Step 5 – Multi-temporal Analysis:** Apply the same workflow to images from **2000, 2010, 2020** to monitor **urban sprawl** and feed into **Smart City Mission and AMRUT** planning. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) — Disaster Management + Forestry Theme** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** Describe the **role of remote sensing in flood management**, explaining the use of **SAR and optical sensors, NDWI, and change detection** techniques. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the application of **NBR and dNBR for forest fire damage assessment**. Illustrate with a worked numerical example. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) Remote Sensing in Flood Management (7 marks)** **Step 1 – Pre-Flood Mapping:** Use archived images (e.g., Sentinel-2, Landsat-8) to map **normal river courses, floodplains, and land cover**. Generate a **DEM from SRTM or Cartosat-DEM** to identify low-lying flood-prone zones. **Step 2 – Real-time Flood Detection:** Use **Sentinel-1 SAR (C-band, VV+VH polarization, 10 m resolution)** because: - Flood events coincide with cloud cover (monsoon) - SAR penetrates clouds - Water produces **very low backscatter** (smooth surface = specular reflection) **Step 3 – Water Extraction using NDWI:** $$ NDWI = \dfrac{\rho_{GREEN} - \rho_{NIR}}{\rho_{GREEN} + \rho_{NIR}} $$ Pixels with $NDWI > 0.3$ are water. **Step 4 – Change Detection:** Compare **pre-flood NDWI** with **post-flood NDWI** or pre/post backscatter intensity: $$ \Delta NDWI = NDWI_{post} - NDWI_{pre} $$ Positive values indicate **newly flooded areas**. **Step 5 – Inundation Depth Estimation:** Combine **DEM elevation** with **water mask** to estimate **inundation depth = DEM elevation − water surface elevation**. **Step 6 – Damage Assessment & Dissemination:** Overlay flood map with **cadastral and population layers** to estimate **affected population, cropland, and infrastructure**. Disseminate via **Bhuvan, ISRO's NDEM portal, and Google Crisis Map**. --- **(b) NBR and dNBR for Forest Fire Damage Assessment (7 marks)** **Step 1 – Definition of NBR:** $$ NBR = \dfrac{\rho_{NIR} - \rho_{SWIR2}}{\rho_{NIR} + \rho_{SWIR2}} $$ where **SWIR-2 = Band 7 of Landsat-8 (2.11–2.29 µm)**. **Step 2 – Physical Basis:** Healthy vegetation has **high NIR (leaf mesophyll scattering)** and **low SWIR (water absorption)** → high NBR. Burned areas invert this trend → low NBR. **Step 3 – Differenced NBR (dNBR):** $$ dNBR = NBR_{pre-fire} - NBR_{post-fire} $$ **Step 4 – Burn Severity Classification (USGS Key, adapted):** | dNBR Range | Severity Class | |---|---| | $\geq 0.66$ | High Severity | | $0.44$ to $0.66$ | Moderate-High Severity | | $0.27$ to $0.44$ | Moderate Severity | | $0.10$ to $0.27$ | Low Severity | | $-0.10$ to $0.10$ | Unburned | | $< -0.10$ | Enhanced Regrowth, Low | **Step 5 – Worked Example:** Pre-fire: $\rho_{NIR} = 0.45$, $\rho_{SWIR2} = 0.18$. Post-fire: $\rho_{NIR} = 0.12$, $\rho_{SWIR2} = 0.30$. $$ NBR_{pre} = \dfrac{0.45 - 0.18}{0.45 + 0.18} = \dfrac{0.27}{0.63} = 0.4286 $$ $$ NBR_{post} = \dfrac{0.12 - 0.30}{0.12 + 0.30} = \dfrac{-0.18}{0.42} = -0.4286 $$ $$ dNBR = 0.4286 - (-0.4286) = 0.8572 $$ Since $dNBR = 0.857 \geq 0.66$, the pixel is classified as **High Severity Burn**. **Step 6 – Application:** Used by **FOREST SURVEY OF INDIA (FSI)** and **Uttarakhand State Forest Department** for post-fire damage assessment and carbon credit accounting. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 4 — Applications** - [ ] **NDVI** is the single most tested index — remember both formula and physical basis (chlorophyll absorption in RED, mesophyll scattering in NIR). - [ ] **NDWI** uses GREEN and NIR — used for **water body detection**; threshold $NDWI > 0.3$ indicates water. - [ ] **MNDWI** uses GREEN and SWIR — better than NDWI for **urban water features**. - [ ] **NDBI** uses SWIR1 and NIR — for **built-up/urban** extraction. - [ ] **Composite Built-up Index (CBI) = NDBI − NDVI**; positive CBI = urban. - [ ] **NBR** uses NIR and SWIR-2 — for **fire damage**; **dNBR = NBR_pre − NBR_post**. - [ ] **SAR** is preferred for **floods** (cloud-penetrating) and **oil spills** (dark spot detection). - [ ] **Thermal IR** is used for **SST, urban heat islands, forest fire hotspots**. - [ ] **Hyperspectral** is used for **mineral mapping and agriculture stress detection**. - [ ] **LiDAR** is used for **canopy height, terrain, and forest biomass**. - [ ] **Accuracy assessment** always uses **Confusion Matrix, Overall Accuracy (OA), and Kappa Coefficient**. - [ ] **ISRO's flagship application missions:** Bhuvan (geospatial platform), FASAL (crop forecasting), NDEM (disaster management), FORMOO (ocean monitoring). - [ ] **Workflow to remember:** Need → Sensor Selection → Acquisition → Pre-processing → Enhancement → Classification → Accuracy Assessment → Product → Decision Support. - [ ] Always quote **numerical values** (resolutions, wavelengths) in your answers — examiners award **bonus marks** for numerical precision. - [ ] **Kappa > 0.80** is the gold standard for accepting any classification in a KTU project. - [ ] For **14-mark answers**, always include a **diagram** (use a labeled block diagram) — it secures 1–2 easy marks. - [ ] For **worked numerical problems**, show **all substitution steps and units** — partial marking requires intermediate steps to be visible.
